con:africa.name: Africa
con:africa.description: South Africa anchors African winemaking with 350 years of history and unique varietals like Pinotage. The Cape's Mediterranean climate and ancient soils produce wines of increasing sophistication. North African nations — Morocco, Tunisia, Algeria — maintain smaller but historic wine cultures dating to Phoenician traders.

con:americas.name: Americas
con:americas.description: New World ambition meets diverse terroir. From Napa's bold Cabernets to Argentina's high-altitude Malbecs, the Americas embrace experimentation while developing distinct regional identities. Chile's coastal valleys, Oregon's cool Willamette, and New York's Finger Lakes prove that geography, not history, defines potential.

con:asia.name: Asia
con:asia.description: Ancient wine cultures and modern ambitions converge. Georgia claims 8,000 years of winemaking with qvevri (clay vessel) traditions. Lebanon's Bekaa Valley has produced wine since Phoenician times. Japan crafts delicate Koshu whites, while China's vast new plantings signal future scale. Israel's boutique revolution proves quality thrives in unexpected places.

con:europe.name: Europe
con:europe.description: The birthplace of fine wine. European winemaking traditions span millennia, from ancient Greek amphorae to French chateau culture. Strict appellation systems — France's AOC, Italy's DOCG, Spain's DO — codify centuries of terroir knowledge. Climate ranges from cool Germanic vineyards to sun-drenched Mediterranean slopes.

con:oceania.name: Oceania
con:oceania.description: Australia and New Zealand have redefined what's possible in warm and cool climates alike. Barossa Shiraz, Margaret River Cabernet, and Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc are now global benchmarks. A willingness to experiment — with screwcaps, with blends, with styles — keeps these regions at the innovation frontier.

typ:fortified.name: Fortified Wine
typ:fortified.description: Wine strengthened with grape spirit, typically to 17-22% alcohol. Port adds spirit during fermentation (preserving sweetness); Sherry adds it after (staying dry). The timing defines the style.

typ:orange.name: Orange Wine
typ:orange.description: White grapes fermented like red wine — with extended skin contact. The result is amber-colored wine with tannin and texture. Ancient in Georgia, revived in Friuli, now made worldwide.

typ:red.name: Red Wine
typ:red.description: Red wine is red because it ferments with grape skins — not because the juice is red. Most red grapes have clear juice. The skins provide color, tannin, and much of the flavor.

typ:rose.name: Rosé
typ:rose.description: Pink wine from limited skin contact — typically hours rather than weeks. The juice is pressed off before it extracts much color. Provence pioneered the pale, dry style now imitated worldwide.

typ:sparkling.name: Sparkling Wine
typ:sparkling.description: The bubbles come from carbon dioxide trapped during a second fermentation. The méthode traditionnelle (used in Champagne) ferments in bottle; the tank method (Prosecco) ferments in steel. The method shapes the mousse.

typ:unknown.name: Unknown Type
typ:unknown.description: Wine type could not be determined from the label or has not been set. You can edit the wine to specify whether it's red, white, rosé, sparkling, orange, or fortified.

typ:white.name: White Wine
typ:white.description: White wine is made by pressing grapes and fermenting only the juice, without skin contact. This preserves freshness and acidity. White wines can be made from white or red grapes — Champagne often uses Pinot Noir.

ctr:ar.name: Argentina
ctr:ar.description: Malbec found its calling at altitude. Mendoza's high-desert vineyards, irrigated by Andean snowmelt, produce wines of concentration and freshness. Argentina is now South America's largest producer.

ctr:au.name: Australia
ctr:au.description: Shiraz found its second home here. Australia's wine industry, established by European immigrants in the 19th century, now emphasizes regional diversity — from cool-climate Tasmania to the ancient soils of the Barossa.

ctr:at.name: Austria
ctr:at.description: Grüner Veltliner and Riesling from the Danube Valley rival Germany's best. Austria's wine laws are strict, its winemakers ambitious. The reds from Burgenland — especially Blaufränkisch — are increasingly celebrated.

ctr:br.name: Brazil
ctr:br.description: Brazil is a South American country known for its diverse climate and geography, which support a variety of viticultural practices. The main wine-producing regions include the Serra Gaúcha, São Paulo, and the Vale dos Vinhedos. Brazilian wine production has gained recognition since the 19th century, with a focus on both table wines and sparkling wines.
ctr:br.style: Notable for both still and sparkling wines, particularly from indigenous and international varieties

ctr:bg.name: Bulgaria
ctr:bg.description: Bulgaria is a country in Southeast Europe with a long history of viticulture, dating back to ancient times. The country is known for its diverse wine regions, which benefit from a variety of climates and soils. Key wine-producing areas include the Thracian Valley, the Danube Plain, and the Black Sea Coast.
ctr:bg.style: Bulgaria produces a range of red, white, and rosé wines, often with indigenous varieties

ctr:ca.name: Canada
ctr:ca.description: Canada is known for its diverse wine regions, primarily located in British Columbia and Ontario. The country has gained recognition for its ice wine production, particularly from the Niagara Peninsula and Okanagan Valley. Canadian wines are characterized by a range of styles, from dry to sweet, and include both red and white varieties.
ctr:ca.style: Diverse styles with a focus on ice wines and cool-climate varietals

ctr:cl.name: Chile
ctr:cl.description: The Andes to the east, the Pacific to the west, desert to the north — Chile's geography creates natural vineyard barriers. Carménère, thought extinct in Bordeaux, thrives here. Maipo Cabernet rivals Napa.

ctr:cn.name: China
ctr:cn.description: China is an emerging wine-producing country with a diverse range of regions suitable for viticulture. Key wine regions include Ningxia, Xinjiang, and Shandong, where both international and indigenous grape varieties are cultivated. The country has a growing wine industry influenced by both traditional practices and modern techniques.
ctr:cn.style: Varied styles including red, white, and sparkling wines

ctr:hr.name: Croatia
ctr:hr.description: Croatia is a country located in Southeast Europe, known for its diverse wine regions and indigenous grape varieties. The country has a long history of viticulture, dating back to ancient times, influenced by various cultures including the Romans and Venetians. Key wine regions include Istria, Dalmatia, and Slavonia, each offering distinct terroirs and wine styles.
ctr:hr.style: Varied styles including reds, whites, and dessert wines

ctr:cy.name: Cyprus
ctr:cy.description: Cyprus is an island nation in the Eastern Mediterranean with a winemaking history dating back over 6,000 years. The country is known for its unique indigenous grape varieties and a range of wine styles, including both red and white wines. Major wine regions include the Troodos Mountains and the coastal areas, with a focus on both traditional and modern winemaking techniques.
ctr:cy.style: Diverse styles including dry, sweet, and fortified wines

ctr:cz.name: Czech Republic
ctr:cz.description: The Czech Republic is a Central European country with a rich history of winemaking, dating back to the Roman era. The primary wine regions are Moravia and Bohemia, with Moravia being the more prominent area for production. The country is known for its white wines, particularly from grape varieties such as Müller-Thurgau and Grüner Veltliner.
ctr:cz.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

ctr:fr.name: France
ctr:fr.description: The template for fine wine worldwide. From Champagne's chalk to Provence's garrigue, France established the vocabulary — terroir, appellation, cru — that the wine world still uses. Most major grape varieties trace here.

ctr:ge.name: Georgia
ctr:ge.description: Georgia is one of the oldest wine-producing countries, with a history of viticulture dating back over 8,000 years. The country is located at the intersection of Europe and Asia, bordered by the Black Sea to the west and the Caucasus Mountains to the north. Key wine regions include Kakheti, Kartli, and Imereti, with indigenous grape varieties playing a significant role in its wine culture.
ctr:ge.style: Varied styles including dry, semi-sweet, and traditional qvevri wines

ctr:de.name: Germany
ctr:de.description: Riesling's homeland, where the grape reaches heights unmatched elsewhere. Steep slate slopes along the Mosel and Rhine produce wines of crystalline purity. The Prädikat system classifies wines by ripeness at harvest.

ctr:gr.name: Greece
ctr:gr.description: Greece is one of the oldest wine-producing countries, with a history that dates back thousands of years. The country features a diverse range of climates and terrains, contributing to a wide variety of wine styles. Key wine regions include Macedonia, Thessaly, and the Aegean Islands, each with distinct grape varieties and winemaking traditions.
ctr:gr.style: Wines range from crisp whites to robust reds, often with unique local characteristics

ctr:hu.name: Hungary
ctr:hu.description: Hungary is a Central European country with a long history of winemaking, dating back to Roman times. The country is known for its diverse wine regions, with Tokaj being the most internationally recognized for its sweet wines made from the Furmint grape. Other notable regions include Eger, known for its red wines, and Villány, which produces robust reds.
ctr:hu.style: Diverse styles including sweet, dry, red, and white wines

ctr:in.name: India
ctr:in.description: India has a developing wine industry, primarily concentrated in the regions of Maharashtra and Karnataka. The country has a diverse climate, ranging from tropical to temperate, which influences viticulture. Historically, wine production in India dates back to ancient times, but modern commercial winemaking began in the late 20th century.
ctr:in.style: Wines range from dry whites to full-bodied reds, often with unique regional characteristics

ctr:il.name: Israel
ctr:il.description: Israel has a diverse wine industry that dates back thousands of years, with modern production beginning in the late 19th century. The country features several key wine regions, including the Galilee, Judean Hills, and Negev Desert, each with distinct climates and terroirs. Major grape varieties cultivated include Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Sauvignon Blanc, reflecting both international and indigenous influences.
ctr:il.style: Varied styles including dry reds, whites, and dessert wines

ctr:it.name: Italy
ctr:it.description: More indigenous grape varieties than any other country. From Alpine Nebbiolo to Sicilian Nero d'Avola, Italy offers staggering diversity. Wine has been made here longer than in France — the Greeks called it Oenotria, 'land of wine.'

ctr:jp.name: Japan
ctr:jp.description: Japan is an island nation in East Asia known for its unique approach to viticulture and winemaking. The country has a long history of rice cultivation, which has influenced its wine production, particularly in the production of sake. Key wine regions include Yamanashi, Nagano, and Hokkaido, where both indigenous and international grape varieties are cultivated.
ctr:jp.style: White and rosé wines, with some red and sparkling varieties

ctr:lb.name: Lebanon
ctr:lb.description: Lebanon is an ancient wine-producing country located in the Levant region of the Middle East. The Bekaa Valley is the primary wine region, known for its Mediterranean climate and diverse terroir. Lebanon has a long history of viticulture, with evidence of wine production dating back over 5,000 years, influenced by various civilizations including the Phoenicians and the Romans.
ctr:lb.style: Red, white, and rosé wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

ctr:lu.name: Luxembourg
ctr:lu.description: Luxembourg is a small landlocked country in Western Europe, known for its wine production primarily in the Moselle region. The country has a long history of viticulture, with evidence of wine production dating back to Roman times. The main grape varieties cultivated include Riesling, Pinot Blanc, and Gewürztraminer.
ctr:lu.style: White wines with a focus on aromatic and crisp profiles

ctr:mx.name: Mexico
ctr:mx.description: Mexico is a wine-producing country with a history that dates back to the 16th century, when Spanish colonists introduced viticulture. The primary wine regions include Baja California, Coahuila, and Guanajuato, with the Valle de Guadalupe being the most prominent. The country has gained recognition for both red and white wines, with a focus on blending traditional European varieties with local adaptations.
ctr:mx.style: Diverse styles including red blends, whites, and sparkling wines

ctr:md.name: Moldova
ctr:md.description: Moldova is a landlocked country in Eastern Europe, bordered by Romania and Ukraine. It has a long history of viticulture, dating back over 5,000 years, and is known for its diverse range of grape varieties and wine styles. The country's wine industry has seen significant development since gaining independence in 1991, with a focus on both traditional and modern winemaking techniques.
ctr:md.style: Varied styles including dry, semi-sweet, and sparkling wines

ctr:nz.name: New Zealand
ctr:nz.description: Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc put New Zealand on the map in the 1980s. The cool maritime climate produces wines of exceptional vibrancy. Pinot Noir from Central Otago and Martinborough rivals Burgundy's best.

ctr:mk.name: North Macedonia
ctr:mk.description: North Macedonia is a landlocked country in Southeast Europe, situated in the central part of the Balkan Peninsula. The country has a long history of viticulture, with archaeological evidence of wine production dating back to ancient times. Key wine regions include Tikveš, Vardar Valley, and Ohrid, with a focus on indigenous grape varieties.
ctr:mk.style: Wines range from dry reds to aromatic whites, often showcasing local varieties

ctr:pt.name: Portugal
ctr:pt.description: Port built the wine trade, but Portugal's diversity extends far beyond fortified wine. Indigenous varieties dominate — Touriga Nacional, Alvarinho, Baga — in a country where modern winemaking meets ancient tradition.

ctr:ro.name: Romania
ctr:ro.description: Romania is a significant wine-producing country in Eastern Europe, with a history of viticulture dating back over 6,000 years. The country features diverse climates and soils across its wine regions, contributing to a wide variety of wine styles. Key wine regions include Transylvania, Moldavia, and Dobrogea, with both indigenous and international grape varieties cultivated.
ctr:ro.style: Varied styles including dry, sweet, and sparkling wines

ctr:rs.name: Serbia
ctr:rs.description: Serbia is a landlocked country in Southeast Europe, located in the central part of the Balkan Peninsula. The country has a long history of viticulture, with evidence of wine production dating back to ancient times. Key wine regions include Vojvodina in the north and the central region of Sumadija, where diverse grape varieties are cultivated.
ctr:rs.style: Serbian wines range from dry reds to aromatic whites and dessert wines

ctr:sk.name: Slovakia
ctr:sk.description: Slovakia is a Central European country known for its diverse wine regions, primarily located in the southern part of the country along the Danube River. The wine industry has a long history, dating back to Roman times, and has seen a revival since the 1990s. Key wine-producing regions include the Small Carpathians, Tokaj, and Nitra.
ctr:sk.style: Varied styles including white, red, and sweet wines

ctr:si.name: Slovenia
ctr:si.description: Slovenia is a Central European country known for its diverse wine regions and rich viticultural history. The country has three main wine regions: Podravje, Posavje, and Primorska, each with distinct climatic and geological conditions. Slovenia's winemaking tradition dates back over 2,000 years, influenced by neighboring countries such as Italy and Austria.
ctr:si.style: Varied styles including whites, reds, and sparkling wines

ctr:za.name: South Africa
ctr:za.description: Wine has been made at the Cape since 1659. Old-bush-vine Chenin Blanc is the signature, but Pinotage (a local cross) and Syrah from Swartland are drawing attention. The industry is transforming rapidly.

ctr:es.name: Spain
ctr:es.description: More vineyard area than any country on earth, though yields are modest in the arid climate. Tempranillo dominates, but old-vine Garnacha and Monastrell are increasingly valued. Spanish wines offer remarkable value.

ctr:ch.name: Switzerland
ctr:ch.description: Switzerland is a landlocked country in Central Europe known for its diverse wine production, primarily in the regions of Vaud, Valais, and Geneva. The country's mountainous terrain and varied microclimates contribute to a wide range of wine styles. Swiss wines are predominantly produced from indigenous grape varieties, with a focus on both red and white wines.
ctr:ch.style: Diverse wines with a focus on indigenous varieties and regional styles

ctr:tr.name: Turkey
ctr:tr.description: Turkey is one of the oldest wine-producing countries, with a history that dates back thousands of years. The country features diverse climates and geographies, allowing for a wide range of grape varieties to thrive. Key wine regions include Thrace, the Aegean, and Central Anatolia, each contributing distinct characteristics to Turkish wines.
ctr:tr.style: Varied styles including red, white, and rosé wines

ctr:gb.name: United Kingdom
ctr:gb.description: The United Kingdom has a growing reputation for wine production, concentrated in the southern counties of England. The maritime climate provides a cooler environment well suited to sparkling wine production. Key wine regions include Sussex, Kent, and Hampshire, with a focus on both traditional-method sparkling and still wines.
ctr:gb.style: Sparkling wines, with some still wines produced

ctr:us.name: United States
ctr:us.description: California dominates production, but quality wine now comes from Oregon, Washington, New York, and beyond. American winemaking combines Old World varieties with New World ambition and technology.

ctr:uy.name: Uruguay
ctr:uy.description: Uruguay is a South American country known for its diverse wine production, particularly in the regions of Canelones, Maldonado, and Colonia. The country has a growing reputation for its quality wines, with a focus on both red and white varieties. Historically, wine production in Uruguay dates back to the 19th century, influenced by European immigrants.
ctr:uy.style: Red wines with a focus on Tannat; also notable whites

geo:es:abona.name: Abona
geo:es:abona.description: Abona is a designated wine region (DO) located in the Canary Islands of Spain. It is characterized by its volcanic soils and diverse microclimates, which contribute to the unique expression of its wines. The region primarily produces white wines, with a focus on indigenous grape varieties that thrive in the local climate.
geo:es:abona.style: White wines with a focus on indigenous varieties

geo:it:abruzzo.name: Abruzzo
geo:it:abruzzo.description: Abruzzo is a region in central Italy, bordered by the Adriatic Sea to the east and the Apennine Mountains to the west. It features a diverse landscape that includes coastal plains, rolling hills, and mountainous areas, contributing to a variety of microclimates. The region is known for its production of red wines, particularly from the Montepulciano grape, and has a long history of viticulture dating back to ancient times.
geo:it:abruzzo.style: Red wines with robust flavors and moderate tannins

geo:cl:aconcagua.name: Aconcagua
geo:cl:aconcagua.description: Aconcagua is a wine region in Chile, located north of Santiago and bordered by the Andes Mountains to the east. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, characterized by warm, dry summers and cool, wet winters, which is conducive to viticulture. Aconcagua is known for its production of high-quality red wines, particularly from the Cabernet Sauvignon, Syrah, and Carmenere grape varieties. The region includes several notable subregions, such as Aconcagua Valley and Casablanca Valley, each with distinct terroirs.
geo:cl:aconcagua.style: Predominantly red wines with a focus on bold flavors

geo:cl:aconcagua-do.name: Aconcagua
geo:cl:aconcagua-do.description: Aconcagua is a prominent wine region in Chile, located north of Santiago. It is characterized by its diverse geography, including the Andes mountains to the east and a coastal influence from the Pacific Ocean. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, with warm days and cool nights, which aids in the development of grape quality. Principal appellations include Aconcagua Valley, Casablanca Valley, and San Antonio Valley, each known for distinct wine styles. The region is recognized for producing high-quality red and white wines, particularly from its cooler coastal areas.
geo:cl:aconcagua-do.style: Diverse wines with a focus on both red and white varietals

geo:tr:aegean-turkey.name: Aegean Turkey
geo:tr:aegean-turkey.description: Aegean Turkey is a prominent wine-producing region located along the western coast of Turkey, characterized by its Mediterranean climate and diverse topography. The region benefits from warm summers and mild winters, with a significant influence from the Aegean Sea. Key wine-producing areas include İzmir and Manisa, where both indigenous and international grape varieties are cultivated. The region is known for its white wines, which often display fresh acidity and aromatic profiles.
geo:tr:aegean-turkey.style: White wines with fresh acidity and aromatic characteristics

geo:pt:algarve.name: Algarve
geo:pt:algarve.description: Algarve is a coastal region in southern Portugal, characterized by a Mediterranean climate with hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters. The region is known for its diverse topography, which includes rolling hills and sandy plains, contributing to a variety of wine styles. Key appellations include Lagos, Portimão, and Tavira, which produce both red and white wines, often from indigenous grape varieties.
geo:pt:algarve.style: Wines range from light whites to robust reds, often with fruit-forward profiles

geo:fr:alsace.name: Alsace
geo:fr:alsace.description: A sliver of France with a Germanic soul. Protected by the Vosges mountains, Alsace is one of France's driest, sunniest regions — and its white wines reflect that generosity. Riesling, Gewurztraminer, and Pinot Gris reach aromatic heights here that few other places can match.

geo:es:andalusia.name: Andalusia
geo:es:andalusia.description: Andalusia is a significant wine-producing region in southern Spain, known primarily for its fortified wines, particularly Sherry. The region's geography features a mix of coastal and inland areas, with a Mediterranean climate that influences viticulture. The principal wine-producing areas include Jerez, Sanlúcar de Barrameda, and El Puerto de Santa María, which are collectively known for their unique solera aging system and diverse styles of Sherry, ranging from dry to sweet. The region has a long history of winemaking, with roots tracing back to ancient civilizations.
geo:es:andalusia.style: Fortified wines, notably Sherry, with various styles and sweetness levels

geo:es:aragon.name: Aragón
geo:es:aragon.description: Aragón is an autonomous community in northeastern Spain, characterized by a diverse geography that includes mountains, valleys, and plateaus. The region has a continental climate, with significant temperature variations between seasons, which influences the viticulture. Key wine-producing areas include Campo de Borja, Cariñena, and Somontano, each known for distinct styles and grape varieties.
geo:es:aragon.style: Red wines, often full-bodied and fruit-forward

geo:nz:auckland.name: Auckland
geo:nz:auckland.description: New Zealand's founding wine region, where Croatian immigrants planted the first commercial vineyards. Waiheke Island, in the Hauraki Gulf, produces Bordeaux-style reds of surprising quality. Kumeu, northwest of the city, excels with Chardonnay.

geo:it:basilicata.name: Basilicata
geo:it:basilicata.description: Basilicata is a region in southern Italy characterized by its mountainous terrain and varied climate, which influences viticulture. The region is home to several wine-producing areas, with Aglianico being the predominant grape variety. Basilicata's wine history dates back to ancient times, with evidence of viticulture from the Greeks and Romans. The region's wines are often noted for their depth and complexity, reflecting the unique terroir of the area.
geo:it:basilicata.style: Red wines, often robust and full-bodied

geo:es:basque-country.name: Basque Country
geo:es:basque-country.description: The Basque Country is an autonomous community in northern Spain, characterized by its rugged coastline and mountainous terrain. The region has a temperate maritime climate, which is conducive to the cultivation of grapes. It is primarily known for its white wines, particularly those produced in the subregions of Getaria and Bizkaia, where the unique terroir influences the wine styles.
geo:es:basque-country.style: Fresh, crisp white wines with high acidity

geo:fr:beaujolais.name: Beaujolais
geo:fr:beaujolais.description: Beaujolais is a wine region located in east-central France, known primarily for its production of red wines made from the Gamay grape. The region is characterized by its rolling hills and granite soils, which contribute to the unique expression of its wines. Beaujolais is divided into several appellations, with Beaujolais AOC and Beaujolais Villages AOC being the most prominent, while the ten Crus of Beaujolais are recognized for their distinct terroirs and higher quality wines.
geo:fr:beaujolais.style: Light-bodied red wines, often with fruity and floral notes

geo:lb:bekaa-valley.name: Bekaa Valley
geo:lb:bekaa-valley.description: The Bekaa Valley is a prominent wine-producing region in Lebanon, situated between the Anti-Lebanon and Lebanon mountain ranges. It benefits from a Mediterranean climate with warm summers and cool winters, which is conducive to viticulture. The region is known for its diverse soil types, including clay, limestone, and gravel, which contribute to the complexity of its wines. Major appellations include the Coteaux de Baalbek and the Coteaux du Liban, where both red and white wines are produced.
geo:lb:bekaa-valley.style: Red wines with rich fruit and spice characteristics

geo:es:binissalem.name: Binissalem
geo:es:binissalem.description: Binissalem is a designated wine region (DO) located on the island of Mallorca in Spain. The region is characterized by its Mediterranean climate, which influences the cultivation of various grape varieties. Binissalem is known primarily for its red wines, with a focus on indigenous grape varieties that reflect the local terroir.
geo:es:binissalem.style: Red wines with a focus on indigenous varieties

geo:cl:bio-bio-valley.name: Bio-Bio Valley
geo:cl:bio-bio-valley.description: Bio-Bio Valley is a wine region located in southern Chile, characterized by its cool climate and diverse terroir. The valley benefits from proximity to the Pacific Ocean, which moderates temperatures and contributes to a longer growing season. It is known for producing high-quality white wines, particularly from varieties such as Chardonnay and Riesling, as well as some Pinot Noir. The region is part of the larger DO (Denomination of Origin) classification system in Chile.
geo:cl:bio-bio-valley.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

geo:cz:bohemia.name: Bohemia
geo:cz:bohemia.description: Bohemia is a wine region located in the Czech Republic, characterized by its varied geography and climate, which influence the viticulture. The region has a long history of winemaking, dating back to the Roman times, and is known for its production of white wines. Key areas within Bohemia include Mělník and Litoměřice, where the combination of river valleys and hilly terrain creates suitable conditions for grape cultivation.
geo:cz:bohemia.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

geo:fr:bordeaux.name: Bordeaux
geo:fr:bordeaux.description: The world's most imitated wine region, built on blending. The Left Bank's gravel soils favor Cabernet Sauvignon; the Right Bank's clay suits Merlot. Centuries of classification have created a hierarchy still debated today. At its best, Bordeaux offers structure, elegance, and remarkable longevity.

geo:fr:burgundy.name: Burgundy
geo:fr:burgundy.description: The spiritual home of Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. Burgundy's patchwork of vineyards — some no larger than a garden — reflects centuries of monastic classification. The region's limestone soils and continental climate produce wines of remarkable transparency, where terroir speaks louder than winemaking.

geo:it:calabria.name: Calabria
geo:it:calabria.description: Calabria is a region located in the southern part of Italy, bordered by the Tyrrhenian Sea to the west and the Ionian Sea to the east. The region features a diverse topography, including mountains and coastal plains, which contribute to its varied microclimates. Calabria is known for its production of red wines, primarily from indigenous grape varieties. The region has a long history of viticulture, influenced by various civilizations over the centuries.
geo:it:calabria.style: Predominantly red wines with robust flavors and moderate acidity

geo:us:california.name: California
geo:us:california.description: California is a prominent wine-producing region in the United States, known for its diverse climates and varied topography. The state encompasses several key wine regions, including Napa Valley, Sonoma County, and Paso Robles, each contributing to its reputation as a significant player in the global wine market. California's wine history dates back to the 18th century, with significant growth occurring in the late 20th century, establishing it as a leader in wine innovation and production.
geo:us:california.style: Varied styles, predominantly red wines with diverse flavor profiles

geo:br:campanha.name: Campanha
geo:br:campanha.description: Campanha is a wine region located in the southern part of Brazil, specifically in the state of Minas Gerais. The region benefits from a temperate climate and diverse soils, which contribute to the cultivation of various grape varieties. Campanha is known for its production of red wines, with a growing reputation for quality in the Brazilian wine industry.
geo:br:campanha.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:it:campania.name: Campania
geo:it:campania.description: Campania is a region in southern Italy, characterized by its diverse geography that includes coastal areas, hills, and volcanic soils. The climate is Mediterranean, with warm summers and mild winters, fostering a variety of grape cultivation. Key wine-producing areas include the provinces of Naples, Avellino, and Salerno, with notable appellations such as Taurasi and Fiano di Avellino. The region is historically significant for its ancient winemaking traditions, dating back to the Greeks and Romans.
geo:it:campania.style: Red wines with robust flavors and aging potential

geo:uy:canelones.name: Canelones
geo:uy:canelones.description: Canelones is a prominent wine-producing region in Uruguay, located just north of the capital city, Montevideo. The region benefits from a temperate climate influenced by the nearby Atlantic Ocean, which helps to moderate temperatures and provides adequate rainfall. Canelones is known for its diverse soils, including clay and sandy loam, which contribute to the complexity of the wines produced here. The region encompasses several notable appellations, including Canelones and Las Brujas, and is recognized for its focus on both red and white wine production.
geo:uy:canelones.style: Red wines with a focus on Tannat and blends

geo:nz:canterbury.name: Canterbury
geo:nz:canterbury.description: Canterbury is a wine region located on the South Island of New Zealand, characterized by its diverse geography and climate. The region benefits from a mix of coastal influences and inland warmth, which supports a variety of grape cultivation. Key areas within Canterbury include the Waipara Valley, known for its distinct terroir and microclimates, and the wider Canterbury Plains. The region is recognized for producing high-quality wines, particularly white varieties.
geo:nz:canterbury.style: Noted for balanced whites and elegant Pinot Noir

geo:tr:cappadocia.name: Cappadocia
geo:tr:cappadocia.description: Cappadocia is a historical region in central Turkey, characterized by its unique geological formations and volcanic soil. The climate is continental, with hot summers and cold winters, which influences the viticulture. The region is known for producing wines primarily from indigenous grape varieties, with a focus on red wines. Cappadocia's wine production has ancient roots, dating back to the Hittite civilization, and continues to be an important part of the local culture.
geo:tr:cappadocia.style: Red wines with moderate tannins and fruit-forward profiles

geo:cl:casablanca-valley-do.name: Casablanca Valley
geo:cl:casablanca-valley-do.description: Casablanca Valley is a wine region located in Chile, within the Aconcagua parent region. It is characterized by a cool coastal climate, influenced by the Pacific Ocean, which is conducive to the production of high-quality white wines. The valley's geography includes rolling hills and a variety of soil types, contributing to the diversity of its wine styles. Casablanca Valley is recognized for its focus on varietals such as Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc.
geo:cl:casablanca-valley-do.style: Known for crisp, aromatic white wines

geo:es:castilla-y-leon.name: Castilla y León
geo:es:castilla-y-leon.description: Spain's high plateau, where extreme temperatures forge concentrated wines. Ribera del Duero and Toro produce some of Spain's most powerful reds from Tempranillo (here called Tinto Fino or Tinta de Toro). Rueda's Verdejo offers crisp refreshment.

geo:es:castilla-la-mancha.name: Castilla-La Mancha
geo:es:castilla-la-mancha.description: Castilla-La Mancha is a significant wine-producing region in central Spain, characterized by its vast plains and diverse climate. It is one of the largest wine regions in the world, known for both red and white wines, with a predominance of red varieties. The region's historical significance dates back to Roman times, and it has seen a resurgence in quality and recognition in recent decades.
geo:es:castilla-la-mancha.style: Red wines with varied styles and increasing quality

geo:es:catalonia.name: Catalonia
geo:es:catalonia.description: From Cava's cellars in Penedès to the steep slopes of Priorat, Catalonia offers Spain's greatest diversity. The Mediterranean climate and varied terrain produce everything from fresh whites to powerful, mineral reds. Barcelona's hinterland is increasingly ambitious.

geo:cl:central-valley.name: Central Valley
geo:cl:central-valley.description: The Central Valley is a major wine-producing region in Chile, characterized by its Mediterranean climate and diverse geography. It encompasses several important wine areas, including Maipo, Rapel, Curicó, and Maule. The region's fertile soils and favorable weather conditions contribute to the cultivation of a wide range of grape varieties, making it a significant contributor to Chile's wine industry.
geo:cl:central-valley.style: Robust reds and fresh whites, often with fruit-forward profiles

geo:md:codru.name: Codru
geo:md:codru.description: Codru is a prominent wine region in Moldova, characterized by its hilly terrain and fertile soils. The region benefits from a continental climate, with warm summers and cold winters, which is conducive to grape growing. Codru is known for its production of white wines, particularly from indigenous and international grape varieties. The region's vineyards are often situated on slopes, allowing for optimal sun exposure and drainage.
geo:md:codru.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and aromatic complexity

geo:cl:colchagua-valley-do.name: Colchagua Valley
geo:cl:colchagua-valley-do.description: Colchagua Valley is a prominent wine-producing region in Chile, located within the larger Rapel Valley. The region is characterized by its Mediterranean climate, which features warm summers and cool coastal breezes, contributing to the cultivation of high-quality grapes. Colchagua is known for its diverse terroir, including varied soil types and altitudes, which allows for a range of wine styles, particularly red wines. The valley is home to several notable appellations and has gained international recognition for its production of premium wines.
geo:cl:colchagua-valley-do.style: Red wines with a focus on bold and fruit-driven profiles

geo:cy:commandaria.name: Commandaria
geo:cy:commandaria.description: Commandaria is a historic wine region located in the Troodos Mountains of Cyprus, known for its production of sweet dessert wines. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, characterized by hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters, which is conducive to the cultivation of grape varieties suited for dessert wine production. Commandaria is one of the oldest named wines in the world, with a winemaking tradition that dates back to ancient times, and it is recognized by the European Union as a protected designation of origin (PDO).
geo:cy:commandaria.style: Sweet, fortified dessert wines with rich flavors

geo:za:constantia-wo.name: Constantia
geo:za:constantia-wo.description: Constantia is a historic wine-producing region located within the Western Cape of South Africa. It is situated on the eastern slopes of the Table Mountain range, benefiting from a maritime climate influenced by the nearby Atlantic Ocean. The region is known for its rich viticultural heritage, dating back to the 17th century, and is particularly recognized for its production of high-quality white wines, especially dessert wines. The terroir features diverse soils and varying altitudes, contributing to the complexity of the wines produced.
geo:za:constantia-wo.style: White wines, including notable dessert styles

geo:fr:corsica.name: Corsica
geo:fr:corsica.description: Corsica is an island region of France located in the Mediterranean Sea, known for its diverse geography that includes mountains and coastal areas. The climate is predominantly Mediterranean, characterized by warm summers and mild winters, which contributes to the cultivation of various grape varieties. The region is recognized for its unique indigenous grape varieties and has a growing reputation for quality wine production, particularly in the Vin de Corse appellation.
geo:fr:corsica.style: Red wines with a focus on indigenous grape varieties

geo:cl:curico-valley.name: Curicó Valley
geo:cl:curico-valley.description: The Curicó Valley is a prominent wine-producing region in Chile, located in the Maule Region. It benefits from a Mediterranean climate with warm days and cool nights, which is conducive to grape growing. The valley is characterized by diverse soils and topography, contributing to a range of wine styles. The region is recognized for its production of both red and white wines, with a focus on varietals such as Cabernet Sauvignon and Sauvignon Blanc.
geo:cl:curico-valley.style: Known for balanced red and white wines with fruit-forward profiles.

geo:gr:cyclades.name: Cyclades
geo:gr:cyclades.description: The Cyclades is an archipelago in the Aegean Sea, known for its unique volcanic soils and Mediterranean climate. The region consists of numerous islands, with Santorini and Naxos being among the most notable for wine production. The Cyclades has a long history of viticulture, dating back to ancient times, and is characterized by its indigenous grape varieties and traditional winemaking practices.
geo:gr:cyclades.style: White wines with mineral and citrus characteristics

geo:hr:dalmatia.name: Dalmatia
geo:hr:dalmatia.description: Dalmatia is a coastal region in Croatia characterized by its Mediterranean climate and diverse geography, including mountains, islands, and coastal plains. The region is known for its rich viticultural history, with wine production dating back to ancient times. Key wine-producing areas include the coastal towns of Split and Zadar, as well as the islands of Hvar and Brač. Dalmatia is recognized for its indigenous grape varieties and a focus on red wines, particularly those made from the Plavac Mali grape.
geo:hr:dalmatia.style: Red wines with robust character and regional diversity

geo:bg:danube-plain.name: Danube Plain
geo:bg:danube-plain.description: The Danube Plain is a significant viticultural region in Bulgaria, characterized by its flat terrain and proximity to the Danube River. The climate is continental, with warm summers and cold winters, which influences the ripening of grapes. The region is known for its production of white wines, particularly from indigenous and international grape varieties. Key appellations within the Danube Plain include Svishtov and Pleven, which contribute to the region's reputation for quality wine production.
geo:bg:danube-plain.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and fruitiness

geo:ro:dealu-mare.name: Dealu Mare
geo:ro:dealu-mare.description: Dealu Mare is a prominent wine region in Romania, located in the central part of the country. It is characterized by rolling hills and a continental climate, which provides favorable conditions for viticulture. The region is known for its red wines, particularly those made from indigenous and international grape varieties. Dealu Mare has a long history of winemaking, dating back to ancient times, and has gained recognition for its quality wines in recent decades.
geo:ro:dealu-mare.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit and structure

geo:hu:eger-dhc.name: Eger
geo:hu:eger-dhc.description: Eger is a prominent wine region located in northeastern Hungary, known for its diverse viticultural landscape. The region features a continental climate with hot summers and cold winters, which contributes to the complexity of its wines. Eger is known for its red wines, particularly those made from the indigenous grape variety Egri Bikavér, or 'Bull's Blood'. The region encompasses several notable appellations, including Eger and Bükkalja, and is characterized by its volcanic soils and hilly terrain.
geo:hu:eger-dhc.style: Red wines with a focus on blends and indigenous varieties

geo:es:el-hierro.name: El Hierro
geo:es:el-hierro.description: El Hierro is one of the smallest and least populated islands in the Canary Islands, Spain. The region benefits from a volcanic landscape and a subtropical climate, which contribute to its unique viticultural conditions. The Denominación de Origen (DO) designation was established to protect and promote the island's wine production, which is characterized by its indigenous grape varieties and traditional winemaking practices.
geo:es:el-hierro.style: White wines with distinctive local character

geo:za:elgin-wo.name: Elgin
geo:za:elgin-wo.description: Elgin is a wine region located within the Western Cape of South Africa, characterized by its cool climate and high altitude. The region benefits from maritime influences due to its proximity to the Atlantic Ocean, which helps to moderate temperatures and extend the growing season. Elgin is known for its diverse terroir, which includes clay, loam, and granite soils, contributing to the complexity of its wines. The region has gained recognition for producing high-quality white wines, particularly from Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc, as well as some notable Pinot Noir.
geo:za:elgin-wo.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and balance

geo:it:emilia-romagna.name: Emilia-Romagna
geo:it:emilia-romagna.description: Emilia-Romagna is a prominent wine region in northern Italy, known for its diverse viticultural landscape. The region features a mix of flat plains and hilly areas, with a climate that varies from continental in the north to Mediterranean in the south. Key appellations include Lambrusco, which is recognized for its sparkling wines, and Colli Bolognesi, known for both red and white varieties. The region has a rich agricultural tradition and is also famous for its culinary heritage, which influences local wine production.
geo:it:emilia-romagna.style: Sparkling wines, with some still red and white varieties

geo:us:finger-lakes-ava.name: Finger Lakes
geo:us:finger-lakes-ava.description: The Finger Lakes region is located in upstate New York and is characterized by its deep lakes and hilly terrain. The climate is influenced by the lakes, which moderate temperatures and create a unique microclimate suitable for viticulture. The region is known for its diverse range of wines, particularly white varieties, and has gained recognition for its cool-climate viticulture. The Finger Lakes AVA includes several notable appellations, each with distinct terroirs.
geo:us:finger-lakes-ava.style: White wines, notable for Riesling and cool-climate varietals

geo:za:franschhoek-wo.name: Franschhoek
geo:za:franschhoek-wo.description: Franschhoek is a prominent wine region located in the Western Cape of South Africa, known for its picturesque valley and rich viticultural history. It benefits from a Mediterranean climate, characterized by warm, dry summers and cool, wet winters, which is conducive to grape growing. The region is home to several wineries and has a strong focus on both red and white wine production, with a particular emphasis on traditional methods and varietals.
geo:za:franschhoek-wo.style: White wines with a focus on Chenin Blanc and Semillon

geo:it:friuli-venezia-giulia.name: Friuli-Venezia Giulia
geo:it:friuli-venezia-giulia.description: Italy's white wine capital, where cool Alpine air meets Adriatic warmth. Pinot Grigio originated here, but the best wines are from indigenous varieties — Friulano, Ribolla Gialla — often with extended skin contact. The orange wine revival started in these hills.

geo:rs:fruska-gora.name: Fruška Gora
geo:rs:fruska-gora.description: Fruška Gora is a prominent wine region located in northern Serbia, characterized by its hilly terrain and continental climate. The region is situated near the Danube River, which influences its microclimate, providing favorable conditions for viticulture. Fruška Gora is known for its historical significance, with vineyards dating back to the Roman era, and it is recognized for its diverse array of white wine production.
geo:rs:fruska-gora.style: White wines with fresh acidity and fruit-forward profiles

geo:es:galicia.name: Galicia
geo:es:galicia.description: Spain's green corner, where Atlantic rain and granite soils produce wines unlike any other in Iberia. Albariño from Rías Baixas is the star — crisp, saline, perfect with seafood. Mencía from inland Ribeira Sacra shows what cool-climate Spanish red can be.

geo:il:galilee.name: Galilee
geo:il:galilee.description: Galilee is a prominent wine-producing region in northern Israel, characterized by its diverse microclimates and varied topography. The region benefits from elevation and cooler temperatures, which contribute to the cultivation of high-quality grapes. Galilee encompasses several subregions, including the Upper Galilee and Lower Galilee, each with distinct terroirs that influence the style of wines produced. The region is known for both red and white wines, with a growing reputation for quality.
geo:il:galilee.style: Red wines with a focus on varietal expression

geo:ch:geneva.name: Geneva
geo:ch:geneva.description: Geneva is a prominent wine region in Switzerland, located along the shores of Lake Geneva. The region benefits from a temperate climate influenced by the lake, which moderates temperatures and provides a unique microclimate for viticulture. Geneva is known for its diverse range of grape varieties and is one of the largest wine-producing regions in the country, with a focus on both white and red wines. The region encompasses several appellations, each with distinct characteristics and styles.
geo:ch:geneva.style: White wines, often fresh and aromatic

geo:nz:gisborne-gi.name: Gisborne
geo:nz:gisborne-gi.description: Gisborne is a wine region located on the east coast of New Zealand's North Island. It is characterized by a warm, temperate climate that is conducive to the production of aromatic white wines. The region is known for its fertile soils and proximity to the Pacific Ocean, which influences its viticulture. Gisborne is recognized for its significant production of Chardonnay and Gewurztraminer, among other varieties.
geo:nz:gisborne-gi.style: White wines, known for fruit-forward and aromatic profiles

geo:il:golan-heights.name: Golan Heights
geo:il:golan-heights.description: The Golan Heights is a prominent wine-producing region in Israel, located in the northeastern part of the country. The region features a diverse topography with volcanic soil, which contributes to the complexity of its wines. The climate is characterized by cool winters and warm summers, allowing for a range of grape varieties to thrive. The Golan Heights is known for its commitment to quality winemaking and has several established wineries producing a variety of styles, particularly red wines.
geo:il:golan-heights.style: Red wines with a focus on bold flavors and structure

geo:si:goriska-brda.name: Goriška Brda
geo:si:goriska-brda.description: Goriška Brda is a notable wine region in Slovenia, located near the border with Italy. The region is characterized by its hilly terrain and a climate influenced by both the Mediterranean and continental weather patterns. Goriška Brda is recognized for its diverse soil types, which contribute to the complexity of its wines. The region primarily produces white wines, with a focus on indigenous and international grape varieties. Key appellations within Goriška Brda include Brda and the surrounding areas, which are known for their high-quality wine production.
geo:si:goriska-brda.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and minerality

geo:es:gran-canaria.name: Gran Canaria
geo:es:gran-canaria.description: Gran Canaria is a volcanic island in the Canary Islands, Spain, known for its diverse microclimates and unique viticultural practices. The region benefits from a combination of coastal and mountainous terrain, which influences grape cultivation. The Denominación de Origen (DO) designation recognizes the quality of wines produced here, particularly red and white varieties.
geo:es:gran-canaria.style: Red wines with notable fruit and mineral characteristics

geo:gb:hampshire.name: Hampshire
geo:gb:hampshire.description: Hampshire is a prominent wine-producing region located in southern England, known primarily for its sparkling wine. The region benefits from a cool maritime climate, which is conducive to the production of high-quality sparkling wines using traditional methods. The chalky soils, similar to those found in Champagne, provide ideal conditions for viticulture. Key areas within Hampshire include the South Downs and the Test Valley, which host numerous vineyards and wineries.
geo:gb:hampshire.style: Produces high-quality traditional method sparkling wines

geo:nz:hawkes-bay-gi.name: Hawke's Bay
geo:nz:hawkes-bay-gi.description: Hawke's Bay is a prominent wine region located on the eastern coast of New Zealand's North Island. The region benefits from a diverse climate, characterized by warm, dry summers and cool winters, which is conducive to viticulture. It is known for its varied topography, including coastal plains and rolling hills, which contribute to a range of wine styles. Key appellations within Hawke's Bay include Gimblett Gravels and Te Mata, recognized for their premium red and white wines.
geo:nz:hawkes-bay-gi.style: Balanced reds and rich whites, particularly Bordeaux blends

geo:de:hessische-bergstrasse.name: Hessische Bergstrasse
geo:de:hessische-bergstrasse.description: Hessische Bergstrasse is a wine region located in the southern part of Hesse, Germany. It is characterized by its warm climate and varied topography, which includes hills and valleys. The region is known for its production of white wines, primarily from the Riesling, Grauburgunder, and Weißburgunder grape varieties. The Hessische Bergstrasse is part of the Anbaugebiet classification, emphasizing its significance in German viticulture.
geo:de:hessische-bergstrasse.style: White wines with a focus on aromatic and fresh profiles

geo:jp:hokkaido.name: Hokkaido
geo:jp:hokkaido.description: Hokkaido is the northernmost region of Japan, characterized by a cooler climate that influences its viticulture. The region features diverse topography, including mountains and plains, which contribute to varying microclimates suitable for grape growing. Hokkaido has gained recognition for its production of white wines, particularly from varieties that thrive in cooler conditions.
geo:jp:hokkaido.style: White wines with crisp acidity and fruit-forward profiles

geo:ge:imereti.name: Imereti
geo:ge:imereti.description: Imereti is a historical region in western Georgia, characterized by its diverse geography, including mountainous areas and fertile valleys. The climate is humid subtropical, which supports a variety of grape cultivation. The region is known for its ancient winemaking traditions, particularly the use of qvevri, a traditional clay vessel for fermentation and aging. Key appellations include Vani and Zestaphoni, which produce distinctive white wines.
geo:ge:imereti.style: White wines, often with a fresh and fruity profile

geo:hr:istria.name: Istria
geo:hr:istria.description: Istria is a prominent wine region located in the northwestern part of Croatia, bordered by the Adriatic Sea. The region features a Mediterranean climate with influences from the continental climate, contributing to a diverse range of grape varieties. Istria is known for its production of white wines, particularly from indigenous grapes, and has gained recognition for its quality and unique terroir.
geo:hr:istria.style: White wines with fresh acidity and mineral notes

geo:cl:itata-valley.name: Itata Valley
geo:cl:itata-valley.description: Itata Valley is a wine region located in southern Chile, characterized by its cool climate and diverse soil types. The valley benefits from proximity to the Pacific Ocean, which moderates temperatures and contributes to a longer growing season. Itata is known for its historical significance in Chilean viticulture, being one of the country's oldest wine-producing areas. The region's focus has shifted in recent years towards the production of high-quality wines, particularly from traditional varieties.
geo:cl:itata-valley.style: Red wines with a focus on traditional varieties

geo:il:judean-hills.name: Judean Hills
geo:il:judean-hills.description: The Judean Hills is a prominent wine region in Israel, located to the west of Jerusalem. The region features a varied topography with elevations ranging from 400 to 900 meters above sea level, contributing to a diverse microclimate. It is characterized by limestone and clay soils, which are conducive to viticulture. The Judean Hills is known for producing a range of red wines, primarily from Bordeaux varietals, as well as some white wines. The region has a growing reputation for quality wine production, supported by both established and emerging wineries.
geo:il:judean-hills.style: Red wines with a focus on Bordeaux varietals

geo:fr:jura.name: Jura
geo:fr:jura.description: The Jura region is located in eastern France, nestled between the Vosges mountains and the Swiss border. It is characterized by a continental climate with significant temperature variations, which influences the grape growing conditions. The region is known for its unique winemaking traditions, including the use of oxidative aging techniques. Key appellations include Arbois, Côtes du Jura, and Château-Chalon, which are recognized for their distinctive styles of white wines.
geo:fr:jura.style: White wines with unique oxidative characteristics

geo:ge:kakheti.name: Kakheti
geo:ge:kakheti.description: Kakheti is a prominent wine region located in eastern Georgia, known for its diverse terroir and rich viticultural history. The region features a mix of mountainous and lowland areas, with a climate that varies from humid subtropical to continental. Kakheti is recognized for its ancient winemaking traditions, particularly the use of qvevri, large clay vessels buried underground for fermentation and aging. The region encompasses several key appellations, including Telavi and Kvareli, which contribute to its reputation for producing distinctive wines.
geo:ge:kakheti.style: Known for orange wines made from indigenous grape varieties

geo:in:karnataka.name: Karnataka
geo:in:karnataka.description: Karnataka is a significant wine-producing region in India, located in the southern part of the country. The region benefits from a diverse climate, ranging from tropical to temperate, which allows for the cultivation of various grape varieties. Key wine-producing areas within Karnataka include the Nandi Hills and the Mysore region, known for their elevation and cooler temperatures, which are conducive to viticulture. The region has gained recognition for its production of sparkling wines and other varietals.
geo:in:karnataka.style: Known for sparkling wines and some still varietals

geo:ge:kartli.name: Kartli
geo:ge:kartli.description: Kartli is a prominent wine region in central Georgia, characterized by its diverse geography and climate, which includes both mountainous and flat areas. The region benefits from a continental climate, with warm summers and cold winters, conducive to viticulture. Kartli is known for its historical significance in Georgian winemaking, being one of the oldest wine-producing areas in the country. The region encompasses several appellations, with a focus on both indigenous and international grape varieties.
geo:ge:kartli.style: White wines with fresh acidity and fruit-forward profiles

geo:gb:kent.name: Kent
geo:gb:kent.description: Kent is a prominent wine-producing region in England, located in the southeastern part of the country. The region benefits from a temperate maritime climate, which is conducive to viticulture. Kent is particularly known for its production of sparkling wines, utilizing traditional methods similar to those of Champagne. The region's chalky soils, particularly in the North Downs, are favorable for growing high-quality grape varieties.
geo:gb:kent.style: Sparkling wines, often made in the traditional method

geo:es:la-palma.name: La Palma
geo:es:la-palma.description: La Palma is a wine region located in the Canary Islands of Spain, characterized by its volcanic soils and diverse microclimates. The region benefits from a subtropical climate, which influences the cultivation of grape varieties. It is classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO), ensuring quality standards for the wines produced here.
geo:es:la-palma.style: Produces white wines with fresh and aromatic profiles

geo:fr:languedoc.name: Languedoc
geo:fr:languedoc.description: Languedoc is a significant wine-producing region in southern France, characterized by a diverse landscape that includes coastal plains, hills, and mountainous areas. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, which contributes to the ripening of grapes and the production of a variety of wine styles. It is known for its red wines, primarily made from blends of Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvèdre, among others. Languedoc has a long viticultural history, with evidence of grape cultivation dating back to Roman times.
geo:fr:languedoc.style: Red blends with fruit-forward profiles and moderate tannins

geo:fr:languedoc-roussillon.name: Languedoc-Roussillon
geo:fr:languedoc-roussillon.description: France's largest wine region by area, and increasingly its most exciting. Once known mainly for bulk production, the Languedoc has reinvented itself. Old-vine Carignan, Grenache from schist hillsides, and ambitious producers are crafting wines that rival France's classic regions at a fraction of the price.

geo:es:lanzarote.name: Lanzarote
geo:es:lanzarote.description: Lanzarote is a wine region located in the Canary Islands, Spain, characterized by its unique volcanic landscape. The region benefits from a warm, dry climate, with trade winds that moderate temperatures. The vineyards are often planted in circular pits to protect the vines from the wind and to capture moisture from the air. Lanzarote is classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO) and is known for its distinctive white wines, primarily produced from local grape varieties.
geo:es:lanzarote.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and minerality

geo:it:lazio.name: Lazio
geo:it:lazio.description: Lazio is a wine region in central Italy, characterized by a diverse geography that includes coastal areas, hills, and volcanic soils. The climate is predominantly Mediterranean, with warm summers and mild winters, which supports a variety of grape cultivation. Key appellations within Lazio include Frascati, Est! Est!! Est!!! di Montefiascone, and Cesanese del Piglio, with a focus on both white and red wines. The region has historical significance dating back to ancient Rome, where wine production was integral to local culture.
geo:it:lazio.style: Primarily white wines, often fresh and aromatic.

geo:cl:leyda-valley-do.name: Leyda Valley
geo:cl:leyda-valley-do.description: Leyda Valley is a wine region located within the Aconcagua Valley in Chile, recognized for its proximity to the Pacific Ocean, which influences its cool climate. The region benefits from a unique terroir characterized by marine breezes and diverse soil types, contributing to the production of high-quality wines. Leyda Valley is particularly noted for its white wine production, especially Sauvignon Blanc, as well as Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
geo:cl:leyda-valley-do.style: Produces crisp white wines and elegant Pinot Noir

geo:it:liguria.name: Liguria
geo:it:liguria.description: Liguria is a coastal region in northwest Italy, characterized by its steep hillsides and proximity to the Mediterranean Sea. The region's climate is Mediterranean, with mild winters and warm, dry summers, which supports the cultivation of various grape varieties. Liguria is known for its white wines, particularly from the Cinque Terre and Golfo dei Poeti areas, where terraced vineyards are common. The region has a rich history of viticulture, influenced by both local traditions and maritime trade.
geo:it:liguria.style: White wines with fresh acidity and mineral notes

geo:cl:limari-valley.name: Limarí Valley
geo:cl:limari-valley.description: The Limarí Valley is a wine-producing region in northern Chile, recognized for its unique terroir characterized by a combination of coastal influences and high-altitude vineyards. The valley benefits from a Mediterranean climate with significant diurnal temperature variation, which aids in the development of acidity in grapes. The region is part of the larger Elqui Province and is classified as a Denomination of Origin (DO). It is known for its production of both white and red wines, with a focus on fresh and aromatic styles.
geo:cl:limari-valley.style: Fresh and aromatic white wines with some red production

geo:pt:lisboa.name: Lisboa
geo:pt:lisboa.description: Lisboa is a wine region located on the western coast of Portugal, characterized by a diverse range of microclimates due to its proximity to the Atlantic Ocean. The region encompasses several subregions, including Alenquer, Arruda dos Vinhos, and Torres Vedras, each contributing unique terroir to the wines produced. Lisboa is known for its production of red wines, primarily from indigenous grape varieties, and has a long history of viticulture dating back to Roman times.
geo:pt:lisboa.style: Predominantly red wines with a focus on fruit and structure

geo:sk:little-carpathians.name: Little Carpathians
geo:sk:little-carpathians.description: The Little Carpathians is a wine region located in western Slovakia, characterized by its hilly terrain and proximity to the Little Carpathian Mountains. The region benefits from a continental climate with warm summers and cold winters, which is conducive to grape growing. It is known for its diverse soil types, including limestone and clay, which contribute to the complexity of the wines produced. The region is home to several notable appellations, including the Bratislava and Pezinok areas, which are recognized for their quality wine production.
geo:sk:little-carpathians.style: White wines with fresh acidity and floral notes

geo:fr:loire-valley.name: Loire Valley
geo:fr:loire-valley.description: France's longest river, France's most diverse wine region. From bone-dry Muscadet at the Atlantic to Sancerre's flinty Sauvignon Blanc, from Chenin Blanc in all its forms to the cool-climate Cabernet Franc of Chinon. The Loire is a living catalog of what French wine can be.

geo:it:lombardy.name: Lombardy
geo:it:lombardy.description: Franciacorta puts Italy on the sparkling wine map — méthode traditionnelle wines that rival Champagne. Valtellina, in the Alpine foothills, produces ethereal Nebbiolo. Milan's region is often overlooked, but quality here is serious.

geo:us:long-island-ava.name: Long Island
geo:us:long-island-ava.description: Long Island is an American Viticultural Area (AVA) located in the southeastern part of New York State. The region is characterized by its maritime climate, which is influenced by the Atlantic Ocean, providing a longer growing season and moderating temperature extremes. Long Island is known for its diverse soil types, including sandy loam and clay, which contribute to the complexity of its wines. The region primarily focuses on red and white wine production, with a growing reputation for high-quality varietals.
geo:us:long-island-ava.style: Red wines with notable Merlot and Cabernet Franc

geo:gr:macedonia.name: Macedonia
geo:gr:macedonia.description: Macedonia is a significant wine-producing region in northern Greece, characterized by its diverse geography and climate, which range from mountainous areas to fertile plains. The region has a long history of viticulture, dating back to ancient times, and is known for its production of both red and white wines. Key appellations include Naoussa and Goumenissa, where indigenous grape varieties thrive alongside international ones.
geo:gr:macedonia.style: Primarily red wines with complexity and aging potential.

geo:uy:maldonado.name: Maldonado
geo:uy:maldonado.description: Maldonado is a wine region located in southeastern Uruguay, characterized by its coastal climate and diverse soils. The region benefits from the influence of the Atlantic Ocean, which moderates temperatures and contributes to a longer growing season. Key appellations within Maldonado include Punta del Este and Garzón, known for their innovative wine production. The region has gained recognition for its red wines, particularly those made from Tannat and other varietals.
geo:uy:maldonado.style: Red wines with a focus on Tannat and blends

geo:gr:mantinia.name: Mantinia
geo:gr:mantinia.description: Mantinia is a designated Protected Designation of Origin (PDO) region located in the central part of the Peloponnese peninsula in Greece. It is characterized by its mountainous terrain and a climate that is influenced by both the Aegean Sea and the inland mountains. The region is primarily known for producing white wines, particularly from the indigenous Moschofilero grape variety, which thrives in the area's unique terroir.
geo:gr:mantinia.style: Primarily aromatic white wines with floral and citrus notes.

geo:it:marche.name: Marche
geo:it:marche.description: Marche is a region located on the eastern coast of Italy, bordered by the Adriatic Sea to the east and the Apennine Mountains to the west. The region features a diverse landscape of hills and coastal plains, contributing to a variety of microclimates suitable for viticulture. Notable wine-producing areas include the provinces of Ancona, Macerata, and Pesaro-Urbino, with a focus on white wine production, particularly from the Verdicchio grape. The region has a long history of winemaking, with evidence of viticulture dating back to ancient Roman times.
geo:it:marche.style: Known for fresh and aromatic white wines

geo:cl:maule-valley-do.name: Maule Valley
geo:cl:maule-valley-do.description: Maule Valley is a prominent wine region in Chile, located in the central part of the country. It is characterized by a Mediterranean climate with a diverse range of soils, which contribute to the production of various wine styles. The region is known for its significant production of red wines, particularly from traditional grape varieties. Maule Valley is recognized for its historical importance in Chilean viticulture, being one of the first areas to cultivate vines in the country.
geo:cl:maule-valley-do.style: Red wines with a focus on traditional varieties

geo:pt:minho.name: Minho
geo:pt:minho.description: Minho is a region in northern Portugal, known primarily for its production of Vinho Verde. The region features a temperate maritime climate, characterized by high rainfall and moderate temperatures, which contributes to the freshness of its wines. The landscape is marked by rolling hills, rivers, and a mix of granite and schist soils, creating diverse terroirs. Minho encompasses several subregions, including Lima, Ave, and Basto, each with distinct characteristics.
geo:pt:minho.style: Fresh, light-bodied white wines with high acidity

geo:de:mittelrhein.name: Mittelrhein
geo:de:mittelrhein.description: Mittelrhein is a wine region located in Germany, characterized by its steep vineyards along the Rhine River. The region benefits from a moderate climate influenced by the river, which contributes to the ripening of grapes. It is known for producing predominantly white wines, with Riesling being the most significant variety. Other notable varieties include Spätburgunder and Müller-Thurgau. The region's unique topography and microclimates create distinct terroirs that influence the wine styles.
geo:de:mittelrhein.style: White wines with a focus on Riesling

geo:ro:moldova-romania.name: Moldova Romania
geo:ro:moldova-romania.description: Moldova is a wine region located in Romania, known for its fertile soil and favorable climate for viticulture. The region benefits from a continental climate, with warm summers and cold winters, which supports the growth of various grape varieties. Key appellations include the wine-producing areas around the cities of Iasi and Cotnari, where traditional winemaking practices are still prevalent.
geo:ro:moldova-romania.style: White wines, often characterized by freshness and fruitiness

geo:it:molise.name: Molise
geo:it:molise.description: Molise is a wine region located in central Italy, bordered by the Adriatic Sea to the east and the Apennine Mountains to the west. The region features a varied landscape of hills and valleys, contributing to diverse microclimates suitable for viticulture. The primary grape varieties cultivated in Molise include Montepulciano and Trebbiano, with a focus on red wine production. The region has a historical significance in Italian winemaking, though it is less prominent than neighboring regions such as Abruzzo.
geo:it:molise.style: Red wines with a focus on Montepulciano

geo:cz:moravia.name: Moravia
geo:cz:moravia.description: Moravia is a prominent wine region in the Czech Republic, located in the eastern part of the country. It is characterized by a continental climate with warm summers and cold winters, which contributes to the development of its vineyards. The region is known for its diverse soil types, including clay, limestone, and sandy soils, which influence the flavor profiles of the wines produced. Moravia is home to several important wine-producing districts, including Znojmo, Mikulov, and Velké Pavlovice.
geo:cz:moravia.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and minerality

geo:lu:moselle-luxembourg.name: Moselle Luxembourg
geo:lu:moselle-luxembourg.description: The Moselle region of Luxembourg is situated along the Moselle River, which forms the border with Germany. This area is characterized by its steep vineyard slopes and a continental climate, which contributes to the production of primarily white wines. The region is known for its production of wines from the Elbling and Riesling grape varieties, among others, and is part of the larger Moselle wine region that extends into Germany.
geo:lu:moselle-luxembourg.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and minerality

geo:es:murcia.name: Murcia
geo:es:murcia.description: Murcia is an autonomous community in southeastern Spain, known for its diverse geography that includes coastal areas and mountainous regions. The climate is predominantly Mediterranean, characterized by hot, dry summers and mild winters, which is conducive to viticulture. The region is home to several important wine-producing areas, including Jumilla, Bullas, and Yecla, primarily known for red wine production from indigenous and international grape varieties.
geo:es:murcia.style: Red wines, often full-bodied with rich fruit flavors

geo:jp:nagano.name: Nagano
geo:jp:nagano.description: Nagano is a prominent wine region located in central Japan, characterized by its mountainous terrain and diverse microclimates. The region benefits from a continental climate, with significant temperature variations between day and night, which aids in the development of grape acidity and flavor complexity. Nagano is known for its production of both red and white wines, with a focus on varietals that thrive in cooler conditions. The region has gained recognition for its wine production since the late 19th century, contributing to Japan's growing wine industry.
geo:jp:nagano.style: Red wines with fruit-forward profiles and moderate tannins

geo:gr:naoussa-pdo.name: Naoussa
geo:gr:naoussa-pdo.description: Naoussa is a wine region located in the northern part of Greece, within the larger region of Macedonia. It is primarily known for producing red wines, particularly from the Xinomavro grape variety. The region benefits from a continental climate, with warm summers and cold winters, which contributes to the complexity and aging potential of its wines. The PDO classification ensures adherence to specific viticultural and winemaking standards.
geo:gr:naoussa-pdo.style: Red wines with high acidity and tannin, often with aging potential.

geo:us:napa-valley.name: Napa Valley
geo:us:napa-valley.description: America's most celebrated wine region, where Cabernet Sauvignon found its New World home. The valley's diverse soils and microclimates — from cool Carneros to warm Calistoga — produce wines of remarkable variety. Napa set the standard for American fine wine.

geo:in:nashik.name: Nashik
geo:in:nashik.description: Nashik is a prominent wine region located in the state of Maharashtra, India. The region benefits from a combination of a favorable climate and diverse soil types, which contribute to the cultivation of various grape varieties. Nashik is often referred to as the 'Wine Capital of India' and has seen significant growth in wine production since the 1990s, with a focus on both domestic consumption and export. The region's elevation and proximity to the Western Ghats influence its viticulture.
geo:in:nashik.style: Produces red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:in:maharashtra.name: Maharashtra
geo:in:maharashtra.description: Maharashtra is India's foremost wine-producing state, accounting for the majority of domestic wine output. The state's wine districts — Nashik, Pune, Sangli, and Solapur — benefit from the Western Ghats' moderating influence, offering elevation-driven temperature variation that aids grape development. Nashik, dubbed the Wine Capital of India, anchors production alongside newer areas such as Pune's Baramati and Solapur's Akluj.
geo:in:maharashtra.style: Fruit-forward reds and aromatic whites; still wines dominant

geo:in:hampi-hills.name: Hampi Hills
geo:in:hampi-hills.description: Hampi Hills is an elevated plateau in Karnataka's Bellary district, rising to approximately 600m and framed by the rocky Deccan landscape around the UNESCO World Heritage Site of Hampi. The significant diurnal temperature variation here — warm days and cool nights — supports slow, even grape ripening. Krsma Estates is the defining producer; their Cabernet Sauvignon and Syrah have attracted international attention.
geo:in:hampi-hills.style: Structured reds with concentrated fruit and firm tannins

geo:in:nandi-hills.name: Nandi Hills
geo:in:nandi-hills.description: Nandi Hills is a mountainous zone in Karnataka approximately 60km north of Bengaluru, with vineyards at elevations exceeding 1,400m — among the highest in Indian viticulture. The altitude ensures cool nights that preserve aromatic intensity and acidity. Grover Zampa has longstanding vineyard holdings here, producing La Réserve and other varietal bottlings.
geo:in:nandi-hills.style: Aromatic whites and structured reds benefiting from high-altitude diurnal shifts

geo:rs:negotin.name: Negotin
geo:rs:negotin.description: Negotin is a wine region located in northeastern Serbia, characterized by its hilly terrain and continental climate. The region benefits from a mix of warm summers and cold winters, which contribute to the ripening of grapes. Negotin is part of the larger Timok Valley wine region and is known for its production of red wines, particularly from indigenous and international grape varieties. The region has a long history of viticulture, with evidence of wine production dating back to Roman times.
geo:rs:negotin.style: Red wines with a focus on indigenous varieties

geo:nz:nelson-gi.name: Nelson
geo:nz:nelson-gi.description: Nelson is a wine region located on the South Island of New Zealand, characterized by its diverse topography and favorable climate for viticulture. The region benefits from a mix of coastal and inland influences, resulting in a range of microclimates suitable for grape growing. Nelson is known for its production of high-quality white wines, particularly Sauvignon Blanc, as well as notable Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. The region's vineyards are often situated on rolling hills and river valleys, which contribute to the complexity of the wines.
geo:nz:nelson-gi.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and fruit expression

geo:gr:nemea-pdo.name: Nemea
geo:gr:nemea-pdo.description: Nemea is a prominent wine region located in the northeastern part of the Peloponnese peninsula in Greece. It is characterized by its mountainous terrain and a Mediterranean climate, which provides ideal conditions for viticulture. The region is primarily known for its red wines, particularly those made from the Agiorgitiko grape, which is indigenous to the area. Nemea has a long history of winemaking, dating back to ancient times, and is recognized for its contribution to Greek wine culture.
