geo:gr:nemea-pdo.style: Red wines, often rich and fruit-forward

geo:au:new-south-wales.name: New South Wales
geo:au:new-south-wales.description: New South Wales is a significant wine-producing region in Australia, known for its diverse climates and varied terroirs. The region encompasses several wine zones, including the Hunter Valley, which is one of the oldest wine regions in Australia, and the cooler climate areas of the Southern Highlands and Hilltops. The state's wine production is characterized by a range of styles, primarily red and white wines, with a focus on both traditional and innovative winemaking techniques.
geo:au:new-south-wales.style: Diverse styles with a focus on red and white wines

geo:us:new-york.name: New York
geo:us:new-york.description: New York is a prominent wine-producing region in the United States, known for its diverse climate and varied topography. The state features several key wine regions, including the Finger Lakes, Long Island, and the Hudson Valley. New York's wine industry has historical significance, with commercial viticulture dating back to the early 19th century. The region benefits from a combination of glacial soils, freshwater lakes, and a range of microclimates that support the cultivation of various grape varieties.
geo:us:new-york.style: Diverse styles, including dry, sweet, and sparkling wines

geo:ca:niagara-peninsula.name: Niagara Peninsula
geo:ca:niagara-peninsula.description: The Niagara Peninsula is a prominent wine region in Canada, located in the southern part of Ontario. It is characterized by a moderate climate influenced by Lake Ontario, which helps to mitigate temperature extremes. The region is known for its diverse terroir, which includes clay, limestone, and sandy soils, contributing to the production of a variety of wine styles, particularly white wines. Key appellations within the Niagara Peninsula include Niagara-on-the-Lake and St. Catharines, which are recognized for their quality and distinctiveness.

geo:at:niederosterreich.name: Niederösterreich
geo:at:niederosterreich.description: Niederösterreich is the largest wine-producing region in Austria, located in the northeastern part of the country. It encompasses several notable wine districts, including Wachau, Kamptal, and Kremstal, which are characterized by their diverse terroirs and microclimates. The region is primarily known for its white wines, particularly those made from Grüner Veltliner and Riesling, reflecting a long-standing viticultural tradition that dates back to Roman times.
geo:at:niederosterreich.style: Dry white wines with high acidity and minerality

geo:cn:ningxia.name: Ningxia
geo:cn:ningxia.description: Ningxia is a prominent wine region located in north-central China, characterized by its arid climate and high altitude. The region benefits from significant temperature variations between day and night, which aids in the development of grape quality. Ningxia has gained recognition for its red wines, particularly those produced from Bordeaux varietals, and has been the focus of investment and development in the Chinese wine industry since the early 2000s.
geo:cn:ningxia.style: Red wines with a focus on Bordeaux varietals

geo:nz:north-island.name: North Island
geo:nz:north-island.description: The North Island of New Zealand is characterized by diverse climates and geographies, influencing its wine production. Major wine regions include Hawke's Bay, Martinborough, and Auckland, each contributing unique varietals and styles. The island is known for its warmer temperatures compared to the South Island, which supports the cultivation of a wide range of grape varieties.
geo:nz:north-island.style: Diverse wine styles, including robust reds and aromatic whites

geo:ca:okanagan-valley.name: Okanagan Valley
geo:ca:okanagan-valley.description: The Okanagan Valley is a prominent wine region in British Columbia, Canada, characterized by its diverse microclimates and varied topography. The valley runs approximately 110 kilometers north to south, bordered by the Okanagan Lake and the surrounding mountains. The region experiences a semi-arid climate, which is conducive to viticulture, with warm summers and cool nights that help preserve acidity in the grapes. Key appellations within the valley include Okanagan Valley, Naramata Bench, and Osoyoos, each contributing unique expressions of wine.
geo:ca:okanagan-valley.style: Red wines with fruit-forward profiles and balanced acidity

geo:us:oregon.name: Oregon
geo:us:oregon.description: Oregon is a prominent wine-producing region in the United States, known for its diverse microclimates and varied topography. The state features several AVAs (American Viticultural Areas), with the Willamette Valley being the most significant for wine production. Oregon's climate is characterized by cool, wet winters and warm, dry summers, which are conducive to growing high-quality grapes, particularly Pinot Noir. The region has gained recognition for its commitment to sustainable viticulture and organic practices.
geo:us:oregon.style: Known for elegant red wines, especially Pinot Noir

geo:za:paarl-wo.name: Paarl
geo:za:paarl-wo.description: Paarl is a prominent wine region located in the Western Cape of South Africa, characterized by its diverse terroir and varied microclimates. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, with warm summers and cool winters, which is conducive to the cultivation of a wide range of grape varieties. Paarl is known for its significant contributions to the South African wine industry, particularly in the production of both red and white wines.
geo:za:paarl-wo.style: A range of red and white wines with notable fruit intensity

geo:mx:parras.name: Parras
geo:mx:parras.description: Parras is a prominent wine region located in Coahuila, Mexico, known for its high-altitude vineyards and arid climate. The region benefits from a unique combination of mineral-rich soils and a significant temperature variation between day and night, which aids in the development of grape quality. Parras is recognized for its historical significance as one of the earliest wine-producing areas in Mexico, with viticulture dating back to the 16th century. The region primarily focuses on red wine production, utilizing both traditional and modern winemaking techniques.
geo:mx:parras.style: Red wines with fruit-forward profiles and balanced acidity

geo:ar:patagonia-ig.name: Patagonia
geo:ar:patagonia-ig.description: Patagonia is a wine region located in the southern part of Argentina, characterized by its cool climate and diverse geography, which includes both mountainous areas and plains. The region benefits from a long growing season with significant temperature variations between day and night, contributing to the development of balanced acidity and flavor in the grapes. Principal appellations within Patagonia include Neuquén and Río Negro, where viticulture has gained prominence since the late 20th century, focusing primarily on high-quality red wines.
geo:ar:patagonia-ig.style: Red wines with a focus on elegance and balance

geo:gr:peloponnese.name: Peloponnese
geo:gr:peloponnese.description: Peloponnese is a prominent wine-producing region in Greece, characterized by its diverse geography and climate. It encompasses several key appellations, including Nemea and Mantinia, which are known for their unique terroirs. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, with warm summers and mild winters, facilitating the cultivation of various grape varieties. The historical significance of Peloponnese in winemaking dates back to ancient times, contributing to its rich viticultural heritage.
geo:gr:peloponnese.style: Primarily red wines with a focus on indigenous varieties.

geo:it:piedmont.name: Piedmont
geo:it:piedmont.description: Italy's answer to Burgundy. In the fog-shrouded hills of the Langhe, Nebbiolo produces wines of haunting complexity — pale in color, immense in structure. Barolo and Barbaresco are the pinnacles, but Barbera and Dolcetto offer daily pleasure. This is Italy at its most aristocratic.

geo:es:pla-i-llevant.name: Pla i Llevant
geo:es:pla-i-llevant.description: Pla i Llevant is a wine region located on the island of Mallorca in Spain, classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO). The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, characterized by warm summers and mild winters, which supports the cultivation of various grape varieties. The diverse soils and topography contribute to the production of wines with distinct characteristics, primarily red wines.
geo:es:pla-i-llevant.style: Red wines with a focus on local grape varieties

geo:ca:prince-edward-county.name: Prince Edward County
geo:ca:prince-edward-county.description: Prince Edward County is a wine region located in Ontario, Canada, situated on the northern shore of Lake Ontario. The region is characterized by a cool climate, which is influenced by the lake's moderating effects, allowing for a longer growing season. The area is known for its limestone and clay soils, which contribute to the unique terroir of the wines produced here. The region has gained recognition for its production of high-quality white wines, particularly from Chardonnay and Pinot Gris.
geo:ca:prince-edward-county.style: Known for crisp, mineral-driven white wines

geo:fr:provence.name: Provence
geo:fr:provence.description: Rosé's spiritual home, though the region offers much more. The pale, dry rosés that have conquered summer tables worldwide are made here in the garrigue-scented hills behind the Côte d'Azur. Ancient vines and ambitious reds are rewriting what Provence can mean.

geo:it:puglia.name: Puglia
geo:it:puglia.description: Puglia is a prominent wine region located in the southeastern part of Italy, bordered by the Adriatic Sea to the east and the Ionian Sea to the south. The region features a Mediterranean climate characterized by hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters, which is conducive to viticulture. Puglia is known for its diverse landscape, including rolling hills and coastal plains, and is one of Italy's largest wine-producing areas. The region is particularly noted for its production of red wines, often made from indigenous grape varieties.
geo:it:puglia.style: Predominantly red wines, often full-bodied with rich fruit flavors

geo:md:purcari.name: Purcari
geo:md:purcari.description: Purcari is a wine region located in the southern part of Moldova, known for its favorable climate and fertile soils. The region has a long history of viticulture, dating back to the 19th century, and is recognized for its production of high-quality red wines. The geography includes rolling hills and a temperate continental climate, which contribute to the development of distinctive wine styles.
geo:md:purcari.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:cl:rapel-valley.name: Rapel Valley
geo:cl:rapel-valley.description: Rapel Valley is a prominent wine-producing region in Chile, located south of the capital, Santiago. It is characterized by a Mediterranean climate with warm days and cool nights, which is conducive to grape growing. The valley encompasses several important appellations, including Colchagua and Cachapoal, and is known for its diverse terroirs that support a range of red wine styles.
geo:cl:rapel-valley.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:cl:rapel-valley-do.name: Rapel Valley
geo:cl:rapel-valley-do.description: Rapel Valley is a significant wine-producing region in Chile, located between the coastal ranges and the Andes mountains. It benefits from a Mediterranean climate with warm days and cool nights, which is conducive to grape growing. The valley is divided into two main subregions: Cachapoal and Colchagua, each known for distinct wine styles and varietals. The region has gained recognition for its red wines, particularly those made from Cabernet Sauvignon and Carmenère.
geo:cl:rapel-valley-do.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:fr:rhone-valley.name: Rhône Valley
geo:fr:rhone-valley.description: A 150-mile corridor along the Rhône River, divided into two distinct wine regions. The steep granite slopes of the north produce powerful Syrah; the sun-drenched plains of the south favor Grenache-based blends. Together they form one of France's most important wine regions.

geo:es:ribera-del-guadiana.name: Ribera del Guadiana
geo:es:ribera-del-guadiana.description: Ribera del Guadiana is a designated Denominación de Origen (DO) located in the western part of Spain. It encompasses a diverse range of landscapes and climates, contributing to the production of various wine styles. The region is characterized by its focus on red wine production, primarily utilizing indigenous grape varieties.
geo:es:ribera-del-guadiana.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward characteristics

geo:za:robertson-wo.name: Robertson
geo:za:robertson-wo.description: Robertson is a wine region located within the Western Cape of South Africa, characterized by its fertile valley and proximity to the Breede River. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, which supports a diverse range of viticulture. It is known for its production of both white and red wines, with a focus on varietals that thrive in warmer conditions.
geo:za:robertson-wo.style: Known for fresh white wines and robust reds

geo:fr:roussillon.name: Roussillon
geo:fr:roussillon.description: France's sunniest wine region, tucked against the Pyrenees and the Spanish border. Grenache and Carignan from old bush vines produce wines of remarkable concentration. The region's fortified Vins Doux Naturels — Banyuls, Maury, Rivesaltes — are among France's most undervalued treasures.

geo:de:saale-unstrut-anbaugebiet.name: Saale-Unstrut
geo:de:saale-unstrut-anbaugebiet.description: Saale-Unstrut is a wine region located in eastern Germany, primarily along the banks of the Saale and Unstrut rivers. The region is characterized by its steep slopes, limestone and slate soils, and a continental climate, which contributes to the production of high-acid wines. Saale-Unstrut is known for its small-scale vineyards and has a history of viticulture dating back to the Middle Ages, making it one of Germany's oldest wine regions.
geo:de:saale-unstrut-anbaugebiet.style: White wines with high acidity and floral notes

geo:de:sachsen-anbaugebiet.name: Sachsen
geo:de:sachsen-anbaugebiet.description: Sachsen is a wine region located in eastern Germany, primarily along the Elbe River. It is characterized by its hilly terrain and a continental climate, which contributes to the production of white wines. The region is known for its small-scale vineyards and a focus on quality over quantity, with a history of viticulture dating back to the Middle Ages. Key appellations within Sachsen include Dresden and Radebeul, which are recognized for their unique terroirs.
geo:de:sachsen-anbaugebiet.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

geo:ar:salta.name: Salta
geo:ar:salta.description: Salta is a wine region located in the northwest of Argentina, characterized by its high altitude vineyards, which can exceed 3,000 meters above sea level. The region benefits from a diverse climate, with warm days and cool nights, allowing for the production of both white and red wines. Salta is particularly known for its Torrontés and Malbec varieties, which thrive in the unique terroir of the area. The region's geography includes mountainous terrain and a mix of sandy and rocky soils, contributing to the distinctive characteristics of its wines.
geo:ar:salta.style: White wines with aromatic profiles and structured reds

geo:ar:salta-ig.name: Salta
geo:ar:salta-ig.description: Salta is a wine region in northwestern Argentina, known for its high-altitude vineyards, which benefit from intense sunlight and significant temperature variations. The region's geography includes the Andes mountains, contributing to its unique terroir. Salta is recognized for producing robust red wines and aromatic whites, with notable appellations such as Cafayate and Molinos.
geo:ar:salta-ig.style: High-altitude wines with intense flavors and aromatic profiles

geo:ar:san-juan-ig.name: San Juan
geo:ar:san-juan-ig.description: San Juan is a prominent wine region in Argentina, located to the north of Mendoza. It features a diverse range of climates, primarily arid, with significant temperature variations between day and night. The region is known for its production of both red and white wines, with a focus on varietals that thrive in its conditions. San Juan has a long history of viticulture, dating back to the Spanish colonization in the 16th century.
geo:ar:san-juan-ig.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward characteristics

geo:us:santa-barbara.name: Santa Barbara
geo:us:santa-barbara.description: California's transverse ranges create east-west valleys that channel Pacific fog inland. The result: some of America's finest cool-climate wines. Sta. Rita Hills Pinot Noir and Santa Maria Chardonnay rival the best of Burgundy. Sideways made it famous; quality keeps it there.

geo:us:santa-cruz-mountains.name: Santa Cruz Mountains
geo:us:santa-cruz-mountains.description: A rugged appellation in the hills above Silicon Valley. Ridge Vineyards put the region on the map; small producers continue the tradition. The mountain vineyards, above the fog line, produce Cabernet and Pinot Noir of unusual structure and longevity.

geo:gr:santorini-pdo.name: Santorini
geo:gr:santorini-pdo.description: Santorini is a prominent wine region located in the Cyclades islands of Greece. The island's volcanic soil and unique climate contribute to the distinctive character of its wines. The region is primarily known for its white wines, particularly those produced under the Protected Designation of Origin (PDO) status, which ensures adherence to specific quality standards and traditional practices.
geo:gr:santorini-pdo.style: Primarily dry white wines with high acidity and mineral notes.

geo:it:sardinia.name: Sardinia
geo:it:sardinia.description: Sardinia is an island region of Italy located in the Mediterranean Sea, known for its diverse geography that includes mountains, hills, and coastal areas. The climate is predominantly Mediterranean, characterized by hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters, which supports a variety of grape cultivation. Key wine-producing areas include Gallura, known for its Vermentino, and the southern region of Cagliari, which produces notable red wines. The island has a rich viticultural history, influenced by various cultures over centuries.
geo:it:sardinia.style: Red wines, often robust and full-bodied

geo:fr:savoie.name: Savoie
geo:fr:savoie.description: Savoie is a wine region located in the eastern part of France, characterized by its mountainous terrain and alpine climate. The region is known for its production of white wines, primarily from indigenous grape varieties. Savoie encompasses several appellations, with a focus on the production of fresh, crisp wines that reflect the unique terroir of the area.
geo:fr:savoie.style: Fresh, crisp white wines with mineral character

geo:br:serra-gaucha.name: Serra Gaúcha
geo:br:serra-gaucha.description: Serra Gaúcha is a prominent wine region in Brazil, located in the southern state of Rio Grande do Sul. It is characterized by its hilly terrain and a temperate climate, which are conducive to viticulture. The region is known primarily for its production of sparkling wines, particularly those made using the traditional method. Serra Gaúcha is home to several important municipalities, including Bento Gonçalves, Garibaldi, and Monte Belo do Sul, which contribute significantly to the Brazilian wine industry.
geo:br:serra-gaucha.style: Sparkling wines, often produced using traditional methods

geo:pt:setubal.name: Setúbal
geo:pt:setubal.description: Setúbal is a wine region located in Portugal, known primarily for its production of fortified wines, particularly Muscatel de Setúbal. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, with influences from the nearby Atlantic Ocean, which contributes to the unique terroir. The region is characterized by its diverse soils, including clay and limestone, which support the cultivation of various grape varieties. Setúbal is classified as a Denominação de Origem Controlada (DOC), ensuring quality and adherence to traditional production methods.
geo:pt:setubal.style: Fortified wines, especially Muscatel de Setúbal

geo:cn:shandong.name: Shandong
geo:cn:shandong.description: Shandong is a prominent wine-producing region located on the eastern coast of China, bordered by the Yellow Sea. The region benefits from a temperate climate, characterized by distinct seasons, which is conducive to viticulture. Key cities within Shandong include Yantai, Qingdao, and Weifang, with Yantai being the most significant for wine production. Shandong is known for its production of both red and white wines, with a focus on varietals that thrive in its climate.
geo:cn:shandong.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward characteristics

geo:it:sicily.name: Sicily
geo:it:sicily.description: The largest island in the Mediterranean, with more indigenous grape varieties than most countries. Nero d'Avola dominates the reds; Grillo and Catarratto produce crisp whites. The volcanic soils of Etna have become Italy's most exciting terroir story. From the western plains to the eastern slopes, Sicily's range is staggering — and increasingly recognised.
geo:it:sicily.style: Diverse styles — red, white, and rosé — anchored by indigenous varieties

geo:hr:slavonia.name: Slavonia
geo:hr:slavonia.description: Slavonia is a historical region in eastern Croatia, characterized by its fertile plains and moderate continental climate. It is known for its production of white wines, particularly from the Graševina grape variety, which thrives in the region's soil and climate conditions. The region has a long viticultural history, with wine production dating back to Roman times, and it is recognized for its traditional winemaking practices.
geo:hr:slavonia.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

geo:us:sonoma.name: Sonoma
geo:us:sonoma.description: Napa's neighbor and rival, with even greater diversity. From foggy Sonoma Coast Pinot Noir to warm Dry Creek Zinfandel, Sonoma's varied terrain produces wines of distinction. Less flashy than Napa, often more interesting.

geo:au:south-australia.name: South Australia
geo:au:south-australia.description: The engine room of Australian wine. From the ancient soils of the Barossa to the cool heights of Adelaide Hills, South Australia produces more than half the country's wine. Shiraz is king, but Riesling from Eden Valley and Clare Valley rivals the world's best.

geo:au:south-eastern-australia-gi.name: South Eastern Australia
geo:au:south-eastern-australia-gi.description: South Eastern Australia is a vast wine region encompassing several states, primarily New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. It features a diverse range of climates and soils, which contribute to the production of a wide variety of wine styles. Significant wine-producing areas within this region include the Riverina, Murray-Darling, and parts of the Adelaide region.
geo:au:south-eastern-australia-gi.style: Diverse styles ranging from robust reds to crisp whites.

geo:nz:south-island.name: South Island
geo:nz:south-island.description: The South Island of New Zealand is known for its diverse wine regions, characterized by varied climates and topographies. The island features several key wine-producing areas, including Marlborough, Central Otago, and Canterbury. The region benefits from a cool maritime climate, which is conducive to the cultivation of high-quality grapes, particularly in the more sheltered valleys and river terraces.
geo:nz:south-island.style: Notable for crisp whites and complex reds, especially Pinot Noir

geo:fr:south-west-france.name: South West France
geo:fr:south-west-france.description: South West France is a diverse wine region located southwest of the Bordeaux region, encompassing various appellations and terroirs. The region features a mix of climates, from oceanic influences to continental conditions, allowing for a wide range of grape varieties and wine styles. Key areas include Cahors, Madiran, and Gaillac, each with distinct characteristics and traditional practices.
geo:fr:south-west-france.style: Red wines, often robust with varied flavor profiles

geo:at:steiermark.name: Steiermark
geo:at:steiermark.description: Steiermark, also known as Styria, is a wine region in southeastern Austria, characterized by its hilly terrain and diverse microclimates. It is primarily known for white wine production, with a focus on fresh and aromatic styles. The region benefits from a continental climate, with warm summers and cold winters, which influences the grape growing conditions. Key appellations within Steiermark include Südsteiermark, Weststeiermark, and Vulkanland Steiermark, each showcasing unique terroirs and varietal expressions.
geo:at:steiermark.style: Aromatic white wines with high acidity and mineral character

geo:gb:surrey.name: Surrey
geo:gb:surrey.description: Surrey is a county in England located to the southwest of London, characterized by a temperate maritime climate that is conducive to viticulture. The region has gained recognition for its production of high-quality sparkling wines, leveraging the same chalky soils found in the Champagne region of France. The establishment of vineyards in Surrey has increased since the late 20th century, contributing to the growth of the English wine industry.

geo:gb:sussex.name: Sussex
geo:gb:sussex.description: Sussex is a prominent wine region in England, located in the southeastern part of the country. The region benefits from a maritime climate, which is influenced by the nearby English Channel, providing favorable conditions for viticulture. Sussex is particularly known for its production of high-quality sparkling wines, utilizing traditional methods similar to those used in Champagne. The region has seen significant growth in vineyard establishment since the late 20th century, contributing to its reputation in the English wine industry.
geo:gb:sussex.style: Sparkling wines, often made using traditional methods

geo:es:tacoronte-acentejo.name: Tacoronte-Acentejo
geo:es:tacoronte-acentejo.description: Tacoronte-Acentejo is a Denominación de Origen (DO) located in the northern part of Tenerife, Canary Islands, Spain. The region is characterized by its volcanic soils and a climate influenced by both Atlantic and mountainous conditions. It is known for producing primarily red wines, with a focus on indigenous grape varieties. The DO encompasses several municipalities, with a landscape that includes terraced vineyards and a mix of coastal and inland areas.
geo:es:tacoronte-acentejo.style: Red wines with a focus on indigenous varieties

geo:au:tasmania-gi.name: Tasmania
geo:au:tasmania-gi.description: Tasmania is an island state of Australia, located south of the mainland. It features a cool maritime climate, which is conducive to the production of high-quality wines. The region is known for its diverse terroir, with a range of soil types and altitudes contributing to the complexity of its wines. Tasmania is recognized for its premium sparkling wines, as well as still wines, particularly those made from Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
geo:au:tasmania-gi.style: Noted for sparkling wines and cool-climate varietals

geo:pt:tavora-varosa.name: Tavora-Varosa
geo:pt:tavora-varosa.description: Tavora-Varosa is a designated wine region in Portugal, recognized for its production of sparkling wines. It is situated in the northeastern part of the country, characterized by its mountainous terrain and a climate that is influenced by both Atlantic and continental factors. The region's vineyards benefit from a combination of altitude and diverse soil types, which contribute to the quality of the grapes grown here. The DOC status was granted to Tavora-Varosa in 1998, highlighting its potential for high-quality wine production.

geo:pt:tejo.name: Tejo
geo:pt:tejo.description: Tejo is a wine region located in central Portugal, bordered by the Tagus River. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate with influences from the river, providing a diverse range of microclimates. It is known for producing both red and white wines, with a focus on red varieties. The region's vineyards are situated on a mix of clay and limestone soils, contributing to the complexity of its wines. Tejo is classified as a Denominação de Origem Controlada (DOC).
geo:pt:tejo.style: Red wines with fruit-forward profiles and moderate tannins

geo:us:texas.name: Texas
geo:us:texas.description: Texas is a significant wine-producing region in the United States, characterized by its diverse geography and climate. It encompasses several viticultural areas, including the Texas Hill Country and the High Plains, which are known for their varying elevations and soil types. The state has a growing reputation for producing quality red wines, particularly from varieties that thrive in warmer climates.
geo:us:texas.style: Primarily red wines with a focus on bold flavors and structure.

geo:tr:thrace-turkey.name: Thrace Turkey
geo:tr:thrace-turkey.description: Thrace is a significant wine-producing region in Turkey, located in the northwestern part of the country, bordering Greece and Bulgaria. The region benefits from a diverse climate, with both continental and Mediterranean influences, which supports the cultivation of various grape varieties. Thrace is known for its red wines, particularly those made from indigenous and international grape varieties, and has a growing reputation for quality wine production.
geo:tr:thrace-turkey.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:bg:thracian-valley.name: Thracian Valley
geo:bg:thracian-valley.description: The Thracian Valley is a prominent wine-producing region in Bulgaria, situated between the Balkan Mountains and the Rhodope Mountains. The region benefits from a continental climate with warm summers and mild winters, which is conducive to viticulture. It is characterized by fertile soils and diverse microclimates, allowing for the cultivation of various grape varieties. The Thracian Valley is known for its production of red wines, particularly from indigenous and international grape varieties.
geo:bg:thracian-valley.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:ch:ticino.name: Ticino
geo:ch:ticino.description: Ticino is a wine region located in the southern part of Switzerland, bordered by the Italian-speaking canton of Ticino. The region is characterized by its hilly terrain and a temperate climate, which is influenced by the nearby Lake Lugano and the surrounding mountains. Ticino is primarily known for its red wines, particularly those made from the Merlot grape, which thrives in the region's warm and sunny conditions. The region's viticulture is supported by a long history of winemaking, dating back to Roman times.
geo:ch:ticino.style: Red wines with a focus on Merlot

geo:mk:tikves.name: Tikveš
geo:mk:tikves.description: Tikveš is a prominent wine region in North Macedonia, located in the central part of the country. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, characterized by hot summers and mild winters, which is conducive to viticulture. Tikveš is known for its diverse soil types, including clay and limestone, which contribute to the complexity of its wines. The region's principal appellations include Tikveš and Kavadarci, with a focus on red wine production.
geo:mk:tikves.style: Predominantly red wines with rich flavors and moderate tannins

geo:hu:tokaj.name: Tokaj
geo:hu:tokaj.description: Tokaj is a historic wine region located in northeastern Hungary, for its production of sweet dessert wines, particularly those made from the Furmint grape. The region benefits from a unique microclimate influenced by the confluence of the Bodrog and Tisza rivers, which creates ideal conditions for the development of noble rot. Tokaj is classified as a DHC (Districtus Hungaricus Controllatus) region, emphasizing its regulated quality and heritage. The region's winemaking traditions date back centuries, contributing to its significance in both Hungarian and European viticulture.
geo:hu:tokaj.style: Known for sweet dessert wines, especially Tokaji Aszú

geo:sk:tokaj-slovakia.name: Tokaj Slovakia
geo:sk:tokaj-slovakia.description: Tokaj is a historic wine region located in eastern Slovakia, known for its production of dessert wines, particularly those made from the Furmint grape. The region benefits from a unique microclimate influenced by the nearby Tisza and Bodrog rivers, which contributes to the development of noble rot, essential for the production of Tokaji Aszú. The region's winemaking tradition dates back centuries, with a significant impact on both Slovak and Hungarian wine cultures.
geo:sk:tokaj-slovakia.style: Known for sweet, botrytized dessert wines

geo:ro:transylvania.name: Transylvania
geo:ro:transylvania.description: Transylvania is a historical region in central Romania, characterized by its diverse geography, which includes mountains, hills, and valleys. The climate is continental, with warm summers and cold winters, contributing to the cultivation of various grape varieties. The region is known for its white wines, particularly from the principal appellations of Târnave and Alba Iulia, where the terroir supports the production of aromatic and fresh styles.

geo:pt:tras-os-montes.name: Tras-os-Montes
geo:pt:tras-os-montes.description: Tras-os-Montes is a wine region in northeastern Portugal, characterized by its mountainous terrain and diverse microclimates. The region is known for its traditional winemaking practices and has gained recognition for its quality red wines. It is situated near the Spanish border and features a range of soil types, contributing to the complexity of its wines.
geo:pt:tras-os-montes.style: Red wines with rich flavors and good aging potential

geo:it:trentino-alto-adige.name: Trentino-Alto Adige
geo:it:trentino-alto-adige.description: Trentino-Alto Adige is a northern Italian region characterized by its mountainous terrain and diverse microclimates. It is known for its production of high-quality white wines, particularly in the southern part of the region. The region's unique geography, with the Alps to the north and the Adige River valley, contributes to a variety of growing conditions that support a range of grape varieties.
geo:it:trentino-alto-adige.style: Aromatic white wines with high acidity

geo:cy:troodos-mountains.name: Troodos Mountains
geo:cy:troodos-mountains.description: The Troodos Mountains are a significant wine-producing region in Cyprus, characterized by high altitudes and a Mediterranean climate. The region's mountainous terrain contributes to diverse microclimates, which are conducive to viticulture. The Troodos Mountains are known for their indigenous grape varieties and traditional winemaking practices, with a focus on red wines. The region's elevation helps to preserve acidity in the grapes, resulting in wines with balance and complexity.
geo:cy:troodos-mountains.style: Red wines with good acidity and structure

geo:it:tuscany.name: Tuscany
geo:it:tuscany.description: Rolling hills, cypress-lined roads, and Sangiovese as far as the eye can see. Tuscany is both Italy's most famous wine region and its most variable — from structured Brunello to the international blends of Bolgheri. The best sites combine clay and limestone soils with perfect sun exposure.

geo:it:umbria.name: Umbria
geo:it:umbria.description: Umbria is a central Italian region characterized by its hilly terrain and a continental climate. It is bordered by Tuscany to the west and Marche to the east, with significant viticultural areas including Montefalco and Orvieto. The region is known for its diverse wine production, particularly red wines made from Sangiovese and Sagrantino grapes, as well as white wines from Grechetto and Trebbiano. Umbria has a rich history of winemaking that dates back to ancient Roman times.
geo:it:umbria.style: Red wines with notable structure and aging potential

geo:ch:valais.name: Valais
geo:ch:valais.description: Valais is a prominent wine region located in the southwestern part of Switzerland, characterized by its mountainous terrain and diverse microclimates. The region benefits from a combination of warm, sunny days and cooler nights, which aids in the development of grape varieties. Valais is known for its unique terraced vineyards along the Rhône River, producing a range of wines, particularly white varieties. The region is one of Switzerland's largest wine-producing areas and has a history of viticulture that dates back to Roman times.
geo:ch:valais.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and minerality

geo:es:valencia.name: Valencia
geo:es:valencia.description: Valencia is a wine region located on the eastern coast of Spain, characterized by a Mediterranean climate that influences its viticulture. The region is known for its diverse topography, including coastal areas and mountainous inland zones. Valencia is classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO) and encompasses several subregions, each contributing to a variety of wine styles, particularly red wines.
geo:es:valencia.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:mx:valle-de-guadalupe.name: Valle de Guadalupe
geo:mx:valle-de-guadalupe.description: Valle de Guadalupe is a prominent wine region located in Baja California, Mexico. It is characterized by its Mediterranean climate, which features warm days and cool nights, conducive to grape growing. The region is known for its diverse topography, including valleys and hills, which contribute to varying microclimates. Valle de Guadalupe is home to several established wineries and has gained recognition for its production of high-quality red wines, particularly from the late 20th century onwards.
geo:mx:valle-de-guadalupe.style: Red wines with a focus on fruit-forward profiles

geo:es:valle-de-guimar.name: Valle de Güímar
geo:es:valle-de-guimar.description: Valle de Güímar is a wine region located in the Canary Islands, Spain. It is classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO) and is situated on the eastern side of the island of Tenerife. The region benefits from a unique microclimate influenced by the surrounding Atlantic Ocean and the volcanic soil, which contributes to the distinct characteristics of its wines. The area is known for its terraced vineyards and traditional winemaking practices.
geo:es:valle-de-guimar.style: Produces white wines with fresh and aromatic profiles

geo:es:valle-de-la-orotava.name: Valle de la Orotava
geo:es:valle-de-la-orotava.description: Valle de la Orotava is a wine region located on the island of Tenerife in the Canary Islands, Spain. It is characterized by its volcanic soils and a diverse microclimate influenced by the Atlantic Ocean. The region is known for its indigenous grape varieties and traditional winemaking practices, contributing to its designation as a Denominación de Origen (DO).
geo:es:valle-de-la-orotava.style: Red wines with a focus on indigenous varieties

geo:ch:vaud.name: Vaud
geo:ch:vaud.description: Vaud is a prominent wine region located on the northern shores of Lake Geneva in Switzerland. The region benefits from a temperate climate influenced by the lake, which moderates temperatures and provides a favorable environment for viticulture. The vineyards are primarily situated on steep terraces, which enhance drainage and sun exposure. Vaud is known for its diverse range of wines, with a particular emphasis on white varieties, notably those produced under the AOC designation.
geo:ch:vaud.style: White wines, often fresh and mineral-driven

geo:it:veneto.name: Veneto
geo:it:veneto.description: Italy's most productive region, stretching from the Alps to the Adriatic. Prosecco and Pinot Grigio fuel volume, but Amarone — dried-grape wine from Valpolicella — is the crown jewel. Soave, from volcanic hillsides, deserves more attention than it gets.

geo:au:victoria.name: Victoria
geo:au:victoria.description: Victoria is a prominent wine region in Australia, known for its diverse climates and topography, which allow for a wide range of grape varieties. The region encompasses several key wine-producing areas, including the Yarra Valley, Mornington Peninsula, and Heathcote. Victoria has a rich viticultural history, with wine production dating back to the 19th century, and it is recognized for both red and white wines, with a particular emphasis on cool-climate varieties.
geo:au:victoria.style: Diverse styles with a focus on cool-climate reds and whites

geo:hu:villany-dhc.name: Villány
geo:hu:villany-dhc.description: Villány is a prominent wine region located in southern Hungary, known for its warm climate and diverse soils. The region is characterized by its limestone and clay terroir, which contributes to the quality of its wines. Villány is recognized for producing predominantly red wines, with a focus on full-bodied styles. The region's historical significance dates back to Roman times, and it has gained recognition for its modern winemaking practices.
geo:hu:villany-dhc.style: Primarily full-bodied red wines with rich fruit and structure.

geo:es:vinos-de-madrid.name: Vinos de Madrid
geo:es:vinos-de-madrid.description: Vinos de Madrid is a Denominación de Origen (DO) located in central Spain, encompassing several municipalities around the capital city of Madrid. The region features a diverse landscape with a mix of clay, limestone, and sandy soils, contributing to the variety of wines produced. The climate is characterized by hot summers and cold winters, with significant diurnal temperature variation, which aids in grape ripening and acidity retention.
geo:es:vinos-de-madrid.style: Red wines, often fruit-forward with moderate tannins

geo:si:vipava-valley.name: Vipava Valley
geo:si:vipava-valley.description: Vipava Valley is a wine region located in southwestern Slovenia, characterized by its diverse microclimates and varied topography. The valley is situated between the Julian Alps and the Karst region, which influences its climate, allowing for both warm and cool air currents. The region is known for its production of white wines, particularly from indigenous grape varieties. Key appellations within the valley include the Vipava appellation, which is recognized for its quality and distinct terroir.
geo:si:vipava-valley.style: White wines with fresh acidity and mineral character

geo:nz:waiheke-island.name: Waiheke Island
geo:nz:waiheke-island.description: Waiheke Island is a wine-producing region located in New Zealand, situated in the Hauraki Gulf near Auckland. The island's geography features rolling hills and a coastal climate, which contribute to its unique terroir. It is known for its production of high-quality red wines, particularly from Bordeaux varieties. The region has gained recognition for its viticulture since the 1970s, with a growing number of wineries establishing themselves on the island.
geo:nz:waiheke-island.style: Red wines with a focus on Bordeaux varieties

geo:nz:waipara-gi.name: Waipara
geo:nz:waipara-gi.description: Waipara is a wine region located within the Canterbury region of New Zealand. It is characterized by a diverse range of soils and a climate that is influenced by the nearby Southern Alps. The region is known for its production of both white and red wines, with a focus on varietals that thrive in its unique terroir.
geo:nz:waipara-gi.style: Noted for balanced whites and structured reds, particularly Pinot Noir

geo:nz:wairarapa.name: Wairarapa
geo:nz:wairarapa.description: New Zealand's answer to Burgundy. Martinborough, at the region's heart, produces Pinot Noir of remarkable delicacy. Small scale, hands-on viticulture, and a challenging climate create wines that reward attention. Ata Rangi and Dry River set the standard.

geo:za:walker-bay-wo.name: Walker Bay
geo:za:walker-bay-wo.description: Walker Bay is a prominent wine region located within the Western Cape of South Africa. It is characterized by a coastal climate influenced by the Atlantic Ocean, which contributes to cooler temperatures and extended ripening periods for grapes. The region is known for its diverse terroir, which includes a mix of sandy, clay, and limestone soils. Walker Bay is recognized for producing high-quality wines, particularly from the Pinot Noir and Chardonnay grape varieties, with notable appellations such as Hemel-en-Aarde Valley and Bot River.
geo:za:walker-bay-wo.style: Red and white wines with a focus on elegance and balance

geo:us:washington.name: Washington
geo:us:washington.description: Washington is a prominent wine region in the United States, known for its diverse climate and geography. The state features a range of microclimates, primarily influenced by the Cascade Range, which creates a rain shadow effect, resulting in a drier climate suitable for viticulture. Key wine-producing areas include the Columbia Valley, Walla Walla Valley, and Yakima Valley, with a focus on both red and white wine production.

geo:au:western-australia.name: Western Australia
geo:au:western-australia.description: Margaret River dominates, but this vast state offers more. The maritime climate of the southwest produces Bordeaux-style blends and Chardonnay of finesse. Great Southern, further east, is emerging with cool-climate Riesling and Shiraz. Isolation breeds distinctiveness.

geo:za:western-cape.name: Western Cape
geo:za:western-cape.description: The Western Cape is a prominent wine-producing region in South Africa, known for its diverse geography and climate. It encompasses several key wine areas, including Stellenbosch, Paarl, and Franschhoek. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, with warm, dry summers and cool, wet winters, which is conducive to viticulture. The varied terroirs support a wide range of grape varieties, contributing to a rich tapestry of wine styles.
geo:za:western-cape.style: Diverse wine styles, including robust reds and crisp whites

geo:at:wien.name: Wien
geo:at:wien.description: Wien, also known as Vienna, is a significant wine region in Austria, characterized by its unique urban vineyards. The region is located in the northeastern part of the country, along the banks of the Danube River. Wien has a continental climate, which is moderated by the river, allowing for the cultivation of various grape varieties. The region is known for its white wines, particularly those produced from Grüner Veltliner and Riesling, and it encompasses several notable vineyards within the city limits.
geo:at:wien.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and minerality

geo:cn:xinjiang.name: Xinjiang
geo:cn:xinjiang.description: Xinjiang is a significant wine-producing region in China, located in the northwest part of the country. It features a diverse geography that includes mountains, deserts, and rivers, contributing to a variety of microclimates suitable for viticulture. The region has a long history of grape cultivation, with modern winemaking practices gaining prominence since the late 20th century. Xinjiang's wine industry is characterized by both traditional and modern techniques, with a focus on red wine production.
geo:cn:xinjiang.style: Red wines with varying profiles and moderate tannins

geo:jp:yamanashi.name: Yamanashi
geo:jp:yamanashi.description: Yamanashi is a prominent wine region located in Japan, situated in the Chūbu region on Honshu Island. It is characterized by its mountainous terrain and a climate that is conducive to viticulture, with warm summers and cold winters. The region is historically significant as it was one of the first areas in Japan to cultivate grapevines and produce wine, dating back to the late 19th century. Yamanashi is home to several key appellations, including Katsunuma and Hōjō, which are known for their production of both white and red wines.
geo:jp:yamanashi.style: Produces white wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

geo:es:ycoden-daute-isora.name: Ycoden-Daute-Isora
geo:es:ycoden-daute-isora.description: Ycoden-Daute-Isora is a wine region located in the Canary Islands, Spain. It is characterized by its diverse volcanic soils and a climate influenced by both the Atlantic Ocean and the surrounding mountains. The region is known for its unique indigenous grape varieties and traditional winemaking practices, contributing to its distinct wine profile.
geo:es:ycoden-daute-isora.style: Produces white wines with local grape varieties

geo:jp:yoichi.name: Yoichi
geo:jp:yoichi.description: Yoichi is a wine-producing district on the western coast of Hokkaido, Japan. Its cool maritime climate, influenced by the Sea of Japan, and volcanic soils produce conditions suited to cool-climate European varieties. Pinot Noir is the flagship grape, alongside Kerner, Zweigelt, and other Germanic varieties. Domaine Takahiko and other small producers have brought international attention to Yoichi as one of Japan's most promising fine wine regions.
geo:jp:yoichi.style: Primarily red wines with a focus on hybrid varieties.

geo:cn:yunnan.name: Yunnan
geo:cn:yunnan.description: Yunnan is a wine region in southwestern China, notable for its high-altitude vineyards in areas such as Shangri-La (Diqing) and Cizhong. The mountainous terrain provides varied microclimates with significant diurnal temperature shifts. The region has attracted attention for unique terroir-driven wines from both international and indigenous grape varieties, with producers exploring Cabernet Sauvignon, Syrah, and local cultivars at elevations exceeding 2,000 meters.
geo:cn:yunnan.style: High-altitude red wines with emerging terroir identity

geo:si:stajerska.name: Štajerska
geo:si:stajerska.description: Štajerska is a prominent wine region located in northeastern Slovenia, characterized by its hilly terrain and diverse microclimates. It is part of the larger Podravje wine district and is known for its production of white wines, particularly from the Sauvignon Blanc and Riesling grape varieties. The region benefits from a continental climate, which contributes to the development of aromatic and crisp white wines. The vineyards are often situated on slopes that enhance drainage and sun exposure, allowing for optimal grape ripening.
geo:si:stajerska.style: Aromatic white wines with high acidity

geo:si:kras.name: Kras
geo:si:kras.description: Kras is Slovenia's karst plateau wine district, stretching from Sežana toward the Gulf of Trieste along the Italian border. Its iron-rich terra rossa soil sits over limestone bedrock, giving structure and dark color to its wines. Kras is the home of Teran, a protected traditional term (PTP) reserved for a high-acid red made from the Refošk grape grown on Kras terra rossa. Vitovska Grganja, an indigenous white grape, is also a local specialty.
geo:si:kras.style: Robust, high-acid reds built around the protected Teran designation.

geo:si:slovenska-istra.name: Slovenska Istra
geo:si:slovenska-istra.description: Slovenska Istra is the Slovenian hinterland of the Istrian peninsula, covering the coastal and inland terrain around Koper, Izola, and Piran along the Adriatic. Its Mediterranean climate and flysch soils support the district's two flagship grapes: Refošk, a deeply colored, high-acid red, and Malvazija Istrska, an aromatic white. Slovenska Istra is a distinct district from Goriška Brda and the Vipava Valley, which lie further north outside the Istrian peninsula.
geo:si:slovenska-istra.style: Refošk-led reds and aromatic Malvazija whites reflecting a Mediterranean coastal terroir.

geo:si:prekmurje.name: Prekmurje
geo:si:prekmurje.description: Prekmurje is Slovenia's northeasternmost wine district, lying east of the Mura river around the Goričko hills near the Hungarian and Austrian borders. Though grouped with Štajerska under the wider Podravje wine region, it is a distinct okoliš with its own character. Prekmurje's signature grape is Modra frankinja (Blaufränkisch), grown alongside the aromatic whites common across Podravje.
geo:si:prekmurje.style: Modra frankinja-led reds alongside Podravje-style aromatic whites.

geo:si:bizeljsko-sremic.name: Bizeljsko-Sremič
geo:si:bizeljsko-sremic.description: Bizeljsko-Sremič lies in eastern Slovenia near Brežice, straddling the Sotla river on the Croatian border, within the Posavje wine region. The district has two defining traditions: Bizeljčan, a light traditional field blend of red and white grapes; and traditional-method sparkling wine (penina) — pioneered here by producers such as Istenič, among Slovenia's oldest bottle-fermented sparkling. Still wines are light and food-friendly.
geo:si:bizeljsko-sremic.style: Two defining styles: the still Bizeljčan field blend and traditional-method sparkling wine (penina).

geo:si:dolenjska.name: Dolenjska
geo:si:dolenjska.description: Dolenjska (Lower Carniola) is a wine district in southeastern Slovenia centered on Novo Mesto, within the Posavje wine region. It is best known for Cviček, a protected traditional term (PTP) for a light-bodied, low-alcohol red-rosé field blend traditionally made from Žametovka (Modra kavčina), Frankinja, Laški Rizling, and Kraljevina.
geo:si:dolenjska.style: Cviček, a low-alcohol red-rosé field blend, is the district's defining designation.

geo:si:bela-krajina.name: Bela krajina
geo:si:bela-krajina.description: Bela krajina (White Carniola) is Slovenia's southeasternmost wine district, centered on Metlika and Črnomelj along the Croatian border, within the Posavje wine region. Its flagship designation is Metliška črnina, a protected traditional term (PTP) for a dark, blended red wine. The district is also known for late-harvest and predikat-style dessert whites.
geo:si:bela-krajina.style: Metliška črnina red blend leads, alongside late-harvest predikat whites.

geo:fr:anjou-saumur.name: Anjou-Saumur
geo:fr:anjou-saumur.description: The Loire's heartland, where Chenin Blanc reaches its peak. Savennières offers bone-dry intensity; Coteaux du Layon provides luscious sweetness. Saumur-Champigny shows that Cabernet Franc can be silky rather than rustic. Remarkable diversity in a compact region.

geo:fr:centre-loire.name: Centre-Loire
geo:fr:centre-loire.description: Sauvignon Blanc's French benchmark. The hills around Sancerre and Pouilly-sur-Loire produce wines of piercing purity — citrus, flint, and herbs in perfect balance. Different soils create distinct styles, but all share that unmistakable tension. Light Pinot Noir reds are increasingly notable.

geo:it:conero.name: Conero
geo:it:conero.description: Conero is a subregion located in the Marche region of Italy, recognized for its production of red wines under the DOCG classification. It is situated along the Adriatic coast, characterized by a hilly landscape that influences the local climate and viticulture. The area benefits from a combination of maritime and continental climates, which contribute to the ripening of grapes. The Conero Riviera is known for its limestone and clay soils, which impart distinct characteristics to the wines produced here.
geo:it:conero.style: Full-bodied red wines with structure and complexity

geo:fr:cote-chalonnaise.name: Côte Chalonnaise
geo:fr:cote-chalonnaise.description: Burgundy's best-value region, south of the Côte d'Or. Mercurey and Givry produce reds that echo the Côte de Beaune at friendlier prices; Rully and Montagny offer excellent white Burgundy. Less famous than its northern neighbors, the Côte Chalonnaise rewards exploration.

geo:fr:cote-de-beaune.name: Côte de Beaune
geo:fr:cote-de-beaune.description: The southern Côte d'Or, where Chardonnay joins Pinot Noir as star. The great white Burgundies — Montrachet, Meursault, Corton-Charlemagne — come from these slopes. Reds from Pommard and Volnay offer elegance over power, while the whites set the global standard for Chardonnay.

geo:fr:cote-de-nuits.name: Côte de Nuits
geo:fr:cote-de-nuits.description: The northern half of the Côte d'Or, running from Dijon to Corgoloin. These slopes produce Burgundy's greatest red wines — Pinot Noir of power, complexity, and longevity. Most of Burgundy's red Grand Crus lie here, from Chambertin to Romanée-Conti. The thin limestone soils and east-facing exposure create wines that age for decades.

geo:fr:cote-de-sezanne.name: Côte de Sézanne
geo:fr:cote-de-sezanne.description: Côte de Sézanne is a subregion of Champagne located to the southeast of Épernay. It is characterized by its predominantly chalky soils and a climate that is slightly warmer than other parts of Champagne. The region is primarily known for the production of Chardonnay, which thrives in the local terroir. The vineyards are situated on slopes that provide good drainage and exposure to sunlight, contributing to the quality of the grapes. Key producers in this area focus on crafting both non-vintage and vintage Champagnes.

geo:es:emporda.name: Empordà
geo:es:emporda.description: Empordà is a subregion within Catalonia, Spain, recognized for its Denominación de Origen (DO) status. It is situated in the northeastern part of the country, bordered by the Mediterranean Sea to the south and the Pyrenees mountains to the north. The region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, characterized by warm summers and mild winters, which supports the cultivation of various grape varieties. The area is known for its diverse terroir, including sandy soils and clay, which contribute to the distinct characteristics of its wines.
geo:es:emporda.style: Red wines with a focus on Garnacha and Cariñena

geo:fr:graves.name: Graves
geo:fr:graves.description: Graves is a subregion of Bordeaux located on the left bank of the Garonne River. It is known for its gravelly soils, which contribute to the quality of its wines. The region is home to both red and white wine production, with a focus on Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot for reds. Graves is also notable for its historical significance, being one of the oldest wine-producing areas in Bordeaux, with a classification system that includes both red and white AOC wines.
geo:fr:graves.style: Red wines are typically full-bodied with structure and aging potential

geo:it:irpinia.name: Irpinia
geo:it:irpinia.description: Irpinia is a subregion within Campania, Italy, recognized for its diverse viticultural landscape. It is characterized by hilly terrain and a continental climate, which contribute to the complexity of its wines. The region is particularly noted for its production of high-quality red and white wines, with a focus on indigenous grape varieties. The DOC classification ensures adherence to specific production standards and regulations.
geo:it:irpinia.style: Red wines, with notable whites from indigenous varieties

geo:it:montefalco.name: Montefalco
geo:it:montefalco.description: Montefalco is a subregion located in the Umbria region of Italy, known for its red wine production. The area is characterized by hilly terrain and a Mediterranean climate, which contribute to the cultivation of high-quality grapes. Montefalco is particularly noted for its Sagrantino grape, which is indigenous to the region and is used to produce robust red wines. The subregion is part of the Montefalco DOC and is recognized for its traditional winemaking practices.
geo:it:montefalco.style: Robust red wines, often with high tannins and aging potential

geo:fr:maconnais.name: Mâconnais
geo:fr:maconnais.description: Burgundy's warm southern reaches, where Chardonnay thrives on limestone slopes. Pouilly-Fuissé leads the way, but Saint-Véran and Viré-Clessé offer similar quality at lower prices. The wines are rounder and more generous than northern Burgundy — less austere, more immediately appealing.

geo:fr:medoc.name: Médoc
geo:fr:medoc.description: Médoc is a subregion of Bordeaux, located on the left bank of the Garonne River. It is characterized by its gravelly soils, which are well-suited for viticulture, particularly for red wine production. The region is home to several appellations, including Margaux, Pauillac, and Saint-Estèphe, known for their high-quality Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot blends. The climate is maritime, influenced by the nearby Atlantic Ocean, which contributes to the ripening of grapes and the development of complex flavors.
geo:fr:medoc.style: Predominantly red wines, often blends with Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot

geo:fr:northern-rhone.name: Northern Rhône
geo:fr:northern-rhone.description: A narrow corridor of steep granite slopes along the Rhône River. Syrah reigns here, producing wines of remarkable depth and longevity. The best sites — vertiginous terraces carved into hillsides — yield wines that combine power with an almost floral elegance, evolving over decades in the bottle.

geo:it:oltrepo-pavese.name: Oltrepò Pavese
geo:it:oltrepo-pavese.description: Oltrepò Pavese is a subregion within the Lombardy region of Italy, recognized for its diverse viticultural landscape. It is characterized by rolling hills and a continental climate, which contribute to the cultivation of various grape varieties. The subregion is noted for its production of both red and sparkling wines, with an emphasis on traditional methods. Key producers in the area focus on quality and the expression of local terroir.
geo:it:oltrepo-pavese.style: Red wines with some sparkling varieties

geo:fr:pays-nantais.name: Pays Nantais
geo:fr:pays-nantais.description: The Loire's Atlantic outpost, where the Sèvre and Maine rivers meet the sea. Melon de Bourgogne — found almost nowhere else — produces Muscadet: bone-dry, briny, and made for oysters. Sur lie aging adds texture to what might otherwise be simple wine.

geo:fr:southern-rhone.name: Southern Rhône
geo:fr:southern-rhone.description: Sun-drenched plains and gentle hills where Grenache thrives. The warmth here produces generous, fruit-forward wines, often blended from multiple varieties. While less austere than the north, the best examples offer surprising complexity — garrigue herbs, dried fruit, and a warmth that lingers.

geo:es:terra-alta.name: Terra Alta
geo:es:terra-alta.description: Terra Alta is a subregion within Catalonia, Spain, recognized for its production of white wines. The area is characterized by a continental climate with Mediterranean influences, which contributes to the ripening of grapes. The subregion is situated in the southern part of Catalonia, with varied topography that includes hills and valleys. Terra Alta is classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO), emphasizing the quality and authenticity of its wines.
geo:es:terra-alta.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and aromatic complexity

geo:fr:touraine.name: Touraine
geo:fr:touraine.description: The garden of France, where châteaux line the riverbanks and Cabernet Franc finds its purest expression. Chinon and Bourgueil produce reds of surprising depth; Vouvray's Chenin Blanc ranges from sparkling to nobly sweet. The Renaissance made Touraine fashionable; the wines keep it relevant.

geo:fr:vitryat.name: Vitryat
geo:fr:vitryat.description: Vitryat is a subregion located within the Champagne region of France. It is characterized by its chalky soils and a climate conducive to the cultivation of high-quality grapes. The area is primarily known for the production of sparkling wines, adhering to the traditional methods of Champagne production. Vitryat benefits from its proximity to the Marne River, which influences the local microclimate.
geo:fr:vitryat.style: Primarily produces sparkling wines using traditional Champagne methods.

geo:it:abruzzo-pecorino.name: Abruzzo Pecorino
geo:it:abruzzo-pecorino.description: Abruzzo Pecorino is a white wine appellation located in the Abruzzo region of Italy, classified as a DOC. The appellation is defined by its legal boundaries, which encompass specific areas within the province of Teramo. The primary grape variety permitted for production is Pecorino, which is known for its distinctive characteristics. The terroir of Abruzzo, with its varied altitudes and coastal influences, contributes to the wine's profile and expression.
geo:it:abruzzo-pecorino.style: Dry white wine with fresh acidity and mineral notes

geo:cl:aconcagua-valley.name: Aconcagua Valley
geo:cl:aconcagua-valley.description: Aconcagua Valley is a designated wine region in Chile, classified as a Denominación de Origen (DO). It is located north of Santiago and is characterized by a Mediterranean climate with warm, dry summers and cool, wet winters. The valley is known for its diverse topography, which includes mountains and valleys that influence the microclimates suitable for viticulture. The region primarily produces red wines, with a focus on varieties such as Cabernet Sauvignon, Carmenère, and Syrah.
geo:cl:aconcagua-valley.style: Primarily red wines with rich fruit and structured tannins.

geo:au:adelaide-hills-gi.name: Adelaide Hills
geo:au:adelaide-hills-gi.description: Adelaide Hills is a Geographical Indication (GI) located in South Australia, known for its cool climate and diverse topography. The region is characterized by rolling hills and a range of altitudes, which contribute to the complexity of its wines. It is recognized for producing high-quality white wines, particularly from Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay, as well as notable Pinot Noir. The region's climate is influenced by its proximity to the ocean, resulting in significant diurnal temperature variation.
geo:au:adelaide-hills-gi.style: White wines with a focus on freshness and acidity

geo:it:aglianico-del-taburno.name: Aglianico del Taburno
geo:it:aglianico-del-taburno.description: Aglianico del Taburno is a Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita (DOCG) located in the Campania region of Italy. It is primarily known for producing red wines made from the Aglianico grape variety. The appellation is situated around the Taburno mountain range, characterized by volcanic soils and a climate that benefits from both Mediterranean and continental influences, contributing to the complexity of the wines.
geo:it:aglianico-del-taburno.style: Full-bodied red wines with high acidity and tannins

geo:it:aglianico-del-vulture.name: Aglianico del Vulture
geo:it:aglianico-del-vulture.description: Aglianico del Vulture is a red wine appellation located in the Basilicata region of Italy, classified as a DOCG. The appellation is named after Mount Vulture, an extinct volcano that influences the local terroir. The wines are primarily produced from the Aglianico grape, which thrives in the volcanic soils and Mediterranean climate of the area. The appellation's regulations stipulate specific vineyard practices and aging requirements to ensure quality. Aglianico del Vulture is known for its structured and age-worthy wines.
geo:it:aglianico-del-vulture.style: Full-bodied red with high acidity and tannins

geo:de:ahr.name: Ahr
geo:de:ahr.description: One of Germany's smallest and northernmost regions, the Ahr Valley is improbably dedicated to red wine. Steep slate slopes along the river create a microclimate warm enough for Spätburgunder, which accounts for over 80% of production. The wines combine Burgundian elegance with German precision.

geo:de:ahr-qba.name: Ahr
geo:de:ahr-qba.description: The Ahr appellation is located in the Ahr Valley in western Germany, characterized by steep slopes and a temperate climate. It is primarily known for its production of red wines, particularly from the Pinot Noir grape. The region's unique geology, with slate and volcanic soils, contributes to the distinctiveness of its wines. The Ahr is one of Germany's smallest wine regions, but it has a long history of viticulture dating back to Roman times.
geo:de:ahr-qba.style: Red wines, with a focus on Pinot Noir and some white varieties

geo:fr:ajaccio.name: Ajaccio
geo:fr:ajaccio.description: Ajaccio is an AOC located in the Corsica region of France, established in 1984. The appellation is situated around the city of Ajaccio, on the western coast of the island. It is characterized by a Mediterranean climate, with influences from the surrounding mountains and the sea. The primary focus of Ajaccio is on red wines, which are predominantly produced from indigenous grape varieties. The region's diverse terroir contributes to the unique characteristics of its wines.
geo:fr:ajaccio.style: Red wines with fruit-forward profiles and moderate tannins

geo:it:albana-di-romagna.name: Albana di Romagna
geo:it:albana-di-romagna.description: Albana di Romagna is a Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita (DOCG) located in the Emilia-Romagna region of Italy. This appellation is exclusively dedicated to the Albana grape variety, which is known for producing white wines. The legal boundaries of the appellation encompass several municipalities within the province of Forlì-Cesena and Rimini, where the climate and soil contribute to the unique characteristics of the wines. The wines produced here can range from dry to sweet, often exhibiting a rich texture and notable acidity.
geo:it:albana-di-romagna.style: White wines, ranging from dry to sweet, with rich texture and acidity.

geo:pt:alenquer.name: Alenquer
geo:pt:alenquer.description: Alenquer is a designated appellation (DOC) located within the Lisboa region of Portugal. It is characterized by a diverse geography that includes rolling hills and proximity to the Atlantic Ocean, which influences its climate. The region is known for producing predominantly red wines, with a focus on indigenous grape varieties. Alenquer has a historical significance in Portuguese viticulture, contributing to the country's wine heritage.
geo:pt:alenquer.style: Red wines with a focus on local grape varieties

geo:pt:alentejo.name: Alentejo
geo:pt:alentejo.description: Portugal's vast southern plains, where cork oaks and olive trees share space with sprawling vineyards. Modern winemaking transformed this region from bulk to quality. Rich, fruit-forward reds from local and international varieties. Portugal's most approachable wines.

geo:pt:alentejo-doc.name: Alentejo
geo:pt:alentejo-doc.description: Alentejo is a designated wine appellation located in the Alentejo region of Portugal. It encompasses a vast area characterized by a Mediterranean climate, with hot summers and mild winters. The appellation is known for its diverse soil types, which include clay, limestone, and sandy soils, contributing to a wide range of wine styles. Alentejo produces both red and white wines, with a focus on indigenous and international grape varieties.
geo:pt:alentejo-doc.style: Diverse wines, primarily red, with rich flavors and moderate tannins

geo:us:alexander-valley-ava.name: Alexander Valley
geo:us:alexander-valley-ava.description: Alexander Valley is an American Viticultural Area (AVA) located within Sonoma County, California. It is characterized by a warm climate with diverse soil types, which contribute to the cultivation of a variety of grape varieties. The AVA is known for its production of high-quality red wines, particularly from Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot, as well as notable Chardonnay. The region's vineyards are situated along the Russian River, benefiting from both river and mountain influences.
geo:us:alexander-valley-ava.style: Red wines with a focus on Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot

geo:es:alicante.name: Alicante
geo:es:alicante.description: Alicante is a Denominación de Origen (DO) located in the Valencia region of Spain. The appellation is characterized by its Mediterranean climate, which influences the ripening of grapes. Alicante is known for producing primarily red wines, with a focus on indigenous and international grape varieties. The region has a historical significance in viticulture, with evidence of wine production dating back to ancient times.
geo:es:alicante.style: Red wines, often rich and full-bodied

geo:es:almansa.name: Almansa
geo:es:almansa.description: Almansa is a Denominación de Origen (DO) located in the Castilla-La Mancha region of Spain. The appellation is characterized by its varied topography and a continental climate, which contributes to the ripening of grapes. It is known for producing predominantly red wines, with a focus on varieties such as Garnacha Tintorera, Monastrell, and Tempranillo. The legal boundaries of Almansa encompass several municipalities, and the region has gained recognition for its quality wine production since its establishment as a DO in 2000.
geo:es:almansa.style: Red wines with rich fruit and moderate tannins

geo:fr:aloxe-corton.name: Aloxe-Corton
geo:fr:aloxe-corton.description: Aloxe-Corton is an AOC located in the Côte de Beaune region of Burgundy, France. It is primarily known for its red wines made from Pinot Noir, which are produced within the legal boundaries of the appellation. The area is characterized by its diverse terroir, which includes a mix of clay and limestone soils that contribute to the complexity of the wines. Aloxe-Corton is situated near the hill of Corton, which is for its Grand Cru vineyards, influencing the quality and style of the wines produced in the appellation.
geo:fr:aloxe-corton.style: Red wines, primarily from Pinot Noir, with depth and structure

geo:fr:alsace-aoc.name: Alsace
geo:fr:alsace-aoc.description: Alsace is an AOC (Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée) located in northeastern France, characterized by its distinct geographic boundaries along the Vosges Mountains. The region is known for its cool climate, which is conducive to the production of aromatic white wines. Alsace primarily produces varietal wines, with a focus on single grape expressions, reflecting the terroir and varietal characteristics. The appellation's regulations dictate specific grape varieties and production methods to ensure quality and authenticity.
geo:fr:alsace-aoc.style: Aromatic white wines, often dry or off-dry, with high acidity

geo:fr:alsace-grand-cru.name: Alsace Grand Cru
geo:fr:alsace-grand-cru.description: Alsace Grand Cru is a classification within the Alsace region of France, designated for wines produced from specific vineyard sites recognized for their exceptional terroir. The appellation encompasses 51 designated grand cru sites, each with distinct geographical and climatic characteristics. The legal boundaries of Alsace Grand Cru are defined by the AOC regulations, which stipulate strict production standards and permitted grape varieties. The wines produced under this appellation are primarily white and are known for their expression of the unique terroir of each grand cru site.
geo:fr:alsace-grand-cru.style: Dry white wines with a focus on varietal purity

geo:it:alta-langa.name: Alta Langa
geo:it:alta-langa.description: Alta Langa is a designated Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita (DOCG) located in the Piedmont region of Italy, primarily known for its production of high-quality sparkling wines. The appellation was established in 2010 and is situated in the hilly areas of the Langhe and Monferrato, characterized by a unique combination of clay and limestone soils. The climate is continental, with significant temperature variations between day and night, which contributes to the development of acidity and complexity in the wines. Alta Langa wines are produced using the traditional method (metodo classico), involving secondary fermentation in the bottle.
geo:it:alta-langa.style: Traditional method sparkling wines with complexity and finesse

geo:it:alto-adige.name: Alto Adige
geo:it:alto-adige.description: Alto Adige is a Denominazione di Origine Controllata (DOC) located in the northern part of Italy, within the Trentino-Alto Adige region. The appellation is characterized by its mountainous terrain and diverse microclimates, which contribute to the production of high-quality white wines. The legal boundaries of Alto Adige encompass various municipalities, with a focus on the valleys and slopes that benefit from both alpine and Mediterranean influences. The region is known for its emphasis on indigenous and international grape varieties, reflecting its unique terroir.
geo:it:alto-adige.style: Aromatic white wines with crisp acidity

geo:it:amarone-della-valpolicella.name: Amarone della Valpolicella
geo:it:amarone-della-valpolicella.description: Wine from raisins. Grapes are dried for months (appassimento) before pressing, concentrating sugars and flavors. The result is powerful, rich, and distinctively bitter (amaro). Corvina dominates the blend. Amarone is Italy's most hedonistic red wine.

geo:us:anderson-valley.name: Anderson Valley
geo:us:anderson-valley.description: Anderson Valley is an American Viticultural Area (AVA) located in Mendocino County, California. It is characterized by a cool climate influenced by coastal fog, which is conducive to the cultivation of cool-climate grape varieties. The region is known for its diverse topography, including rolling hills and valleys, which contribute to a range of microclimates. Anderson Valley is recognized for producing high-quality wines, particularly from Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, as well as aromatic varieties like Gewurztraminer.
geo:us:anderson-valley.style: Red wines with notable acidity and fruit expression

geo:fr:anjou-aoc.name: Anjou
geo:fr:anjou-aoc.description: Anjou is an AOC located within the Anjou-Saumur region of France, known primarily for its white wines. The appellation encompasses a variety of terroirs, characterized by schist, clay, and limestone soils, which contribute to the diversity of its wine styles. Anjou wines are typically produced from both traditional and modern vinification techniques, reflecting the region's historical significance in the Loire Valley wine production.
geo:fr:anjou-aoc.style: White wines, often dry or off-dry, with notable acidity

geo:fr:anjou-villages.name: Anjou-Villages
geo:fr:anjou-villages.description: Anjou-Villages is an AOC located within the Anjou-Saumur region of France, primarily known for producing red wines. The appellation is characterized by its specific legal boundaries that encompass several communes in the Loire Valley. Anjou-Villages wines are predominantly made from Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon, showcasing the region's unique terroir, which includes a mix of clay and limestone soils. The climate is temperate, influenced by the Loire River, contributing to the ripening of grapes and the development of wine styles.
geo:fr:anjou-villages.style: Red wines with a focus on Cabernet varieties

geo:fr:anjou-villages-brissac.name: Anjou-Villages Brissac
geo:fr:anjou-villages-brissac.description: Anjou-Villages Brissac is an AOC located within the Anjou-Saumur region of France. It is primarily known for producing red wines made from Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon. The appellation is characterized by its distinct terroir, which includes clay and limestone soils that contribute to the complexity of the wines. The legal boundaries of Anjou-Villages Brissac encompass specific communes that are recognized for their suitability for viticulture, ensuring the production of high-quality wines.
geo:fr:anjou-villages-brissac.style: Red wines with structure and aging potential

geo:es:arabako-txakolina.name: Arabako Txakolina
geo:es:arabako-txakolina.description: Arabako Txakolina is a designated wine appellation (DO) located in the Basque Country of Spain. It is characterized by its production of white wines primarily from the indigenous Hondarrabi Zuri grape variety. The region benefits from a humid oceanic climate, which influences the acidity and freshness of the wines.
