var:juan-garcia.description: Juan García is a red grape variety native to the Arribes del Duero region along the Spanish-Portuguese border. It produces dark, structured wines with blackberry and mineral notes, well-suited to the region's dramatic terraced riverside vineyards. The grape is important to the Arribes DO and represents the viticultural heritage of this remote border region.

var:rufete.name: Rufete
var:rufete.description: Rufete is a red grape variety found in the Arribes del Duero and Sierra de Salamanca wine regions of western Spain. It produces light to medium-bodied, aromatic red wines with bright acidity. The grape was once widely planted in the region but declined; it is now being revived by producers recognizing its distinct character.
var:rufete.style: Aromatic, light Salamanca red, undergoing revival

var:prieto-picudo.name: Prieto Picudo
var:prieto-picudo.description: Prieto Picudo is a red grape variety indigenous to the Tierra de León DO in northwestern Spain. Named for its small, dark, pointed berries, it produces deeply colored wines with firm tannins and dark fruit. The grape is particularly known for producing a distinctive rosado (rosé) using a traditional maceration method called 'madreo.'
var:prieto-picudo.style: Dark, tannic León red, famous for traditional rosé

var:albarin.name: Albarín
var:albarin.description: Albarín (or Albarín Blanco) is a white grape variety native to León in northwestern Spain. It produces aromatic, floral white wines with citrus and stone fruit notes. Though rare, Albarín is increasingly valued for its quality potential and represents the diversity of indigenous Spanish white varieties.
var:albarin.style: Aromatic León white, rare and increasingly valued

var:trepat.name: Trepat
var:trepat.description: Trepat is a red grape variety grown primarily in the Conca de Barberà DO of Catalonia. It is used for both rosé wines and as a component in Cava production. Trepat produces light-colored, fresh wines with red berry fruit and floral notes. It is one of the few red varieties officially permitted in Cava production.
var:trepat.style: Light Catalan rosé and Cava grape

var:picapoll.name: Picapoll
var:picapoll.description: Picapoll (or Picapoll Blanc) is a white grape variety native to the Pla de Bages DO in central Catalonia. It produces crisp, mineral-driven white wines with citrus and herbal notes. The grape is experiencing a revival as the Pla de Bages region gains recognition for its distinctive terroir.

var:sumoll.name: Sumoll
var:sumoll.description: Sumoll is a red grape variety indigenous to Catalonia, particularly associated with the Pla de Bages and Penedès regions. Nearly extinct in the late 20th century, Sumoll has been revived by natural wine producers and quality-focused estates. It produces tannic, structured wines with dark fruit and herbal notes.
var:sumoll.style: Tannic, revived Catalan red, natural wine favorite

var:zalema.name: Zaléma
var:zalema.description: Zaléma is a white grape variety grown primarily in the Condado de Huelva DO in southwestern Spain. It is the dominant grape in this warm Atlantic-influenced region, producing neutral, light-bodied whites. Zaléma is also used in the production of local fortified wines.
var:zalema.style: Neutral Huelva white, local Spanish specialty

var:rome.name: Romé
var:rome.description: Romé is a rare red grape variety found in the Sierras de Málaga wine region of southern Spain. It produces deeply colored wines and is part of the small but diverse collection of indigenous grape varieties being preserved in Andalucía.

var:verijadiego.name: Verijadiego
var:verijadiego.description: Verijadiego is a white grape variety found on the Canary Island of El Hierro, the smallest and most remote of the main Canary Islands. It produces aromatic, mineral white wines reflecting the extreme volcanic terroir. The grape is extremely rare and represents the unique viticultural heritage of these isolated Atlantic islands.

var:baboso-negro.name: Baboso Negro
var:baboso-negro.description: Baboso Negro is a rare red grape variety found on the Canary Islands, particularly El Hierro. It produces deeply colored wines with dark fruit and volcanic mineral character. Growing on pre-phylloxera vines in volcanic soils, Baboso Negro wines are increasingly sought by collectors interested in the unique terroir of the Canary Islands.
var:baboso-negro.style: Rare, dark volcanic Canary Islands red

var:diego.name: Diego
var:diego.description: Diego is a white grape variety indigenous to Lanzarote in the Canary Islands, where it is grown in the dramatic 'zocos' — individual vine hollows dug into volcanic ash. It produces fresh, mineral-driven white wines reflecting the island's extreme volcanic terroir and Atlantic influence.
var:diego.style: Indigenous Lanzarote white, volcanic hollows grape

var:garnacha-tintorera.name: Garnacha Tintorera
var:garnacha-tintorera.description: Garnacha Tintorera (known internationally as Alicante Bouschet) is a teinturier grape — one of very few varieties with red flesh as well as red skin. Created in 19th-century France by Henri Bouschet, it is now widely planted in Spain's Almansa, Alicante, and Ribeira Sacra regions. It produces deeply colored, full-bodied wines and is valued for adding color intensity to blends.
var:garnacha-tintorera.style: Teinturier grape with red flesh, deep color specialist

var:treixadura.name: Treixadura
var:treixadura.description: Treixadura is a white grape variety native to Galicia in northwestern Spain, particularly associated with the Ribeiro DO where it is considered the finest local variety. It produces aromatic, complex white wines with stone fruit, floral, and citrus notes. The grape also appears in Rías Baixas blends.
var:treixadura.style: Aromatic Galician white, Ribeiro's star grape

var:vijariego.name: Vijariego
var:vijariego.description: Vijariego is a white grape variety found in the Canary Islands, particularly in Ycoden-Daute-Isora on Tenerife. It produces fresh, mineral white wines. The grape is part of the rich tapestry of indigenous Canary Island varieties growing on volcanic soils at altitude.

var:merseguera.name: Merseguera
var:merseguera.description: Merseguera is a white grape variety grown in the Valencia and Alicante wine regions of eastern Spain. It produces light, neutral white wines with moderate acidity. Well-adapted to hot, dry Mediterranean conditions, Merseguera is drought-resistant and one of the traditional varieties of the Levante coast.

var:pardina.name: Pardina
var:pardina.description: Pardina (also known as Pardillo) is a white grape variety found primarily in Extremadura, western Spain. It produces neutral, light-bodied white wines and is one of the region's most planted varieties. Like many hot-climate Spanish whites, it is valued for its reliability in dry conditions.

var:cayetana-blanca.name: Cayetana Blanca
var:cayetana-blanca.description: Cayetana Blanca is a high-yielding white grape variety grown primarily in Extremadura, Spain. It produces neutral, light-bodied wines and has historically been used for bulk production and brandy distillation. It is one of Spain's most planted white varieties by area, concentrated in the Ribera del Guadiana DO.
var:cayetana-blanca.style: High-yield Extremadura white, bulk and brandy grape

var:pais.name: País
var:pais.description: País (known as Mission in California and Listán Prieto in the Canary Islands) is a historic red grape variety brought to the Americas by Spanish missionaries in the 16th century. In Chile's Maule and Itata Valleys, ancient País vines — some over a century old — are being rediscovered by a new generation of winemakers producing light, vibrant reds from dry-farmed bush vines. These wines offer a taste of history.
var:pais.style: Historic missionary grape, revived Chilean old-vine specialty

var:dornfelder.name: Dornfelder
var:dornfelder.description: Dornfelder is a red grape variety created in 1955 at the Weinsberg grape breeding institute in Württemberg, Germany. A cross of Helfensteiner and Heroldrebe, it has become Germany's most planted red variety after Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir). Dornfelder produces deeply colored, fruity red wines with soft tannins, filling a niche for approachable German reds. It is found across most German wine regions.
var:dornfelder.style: Germany's popular modern red crossing, deeply colored and fruity

var:ramisco.name: Ramisco
var:ramisco.description: Ramisco is a rare red grape variety found almost exclusively in the Colares wine region near Lisbon, where ungrafted vines grow in sandy soils that protected them from the phylloxera epidemic. Ramisco produces tannic, age-worthy wines that require long aging. The grape and the Colares region represent one of Europe's most remarkable viticultural survivals.
var:ramisco.style: Rare phylloxera-survivor, tannic Colares red on ungrafted vines

var:galego-dourado.name: Galego Dourado
var:galego-dourado.description: Galego Dourado is a rare white grape variety found in the Carcavelos region near Lisbon, Portugal. It is used in the production of the traditional fortified wine of Carcavelos. Very few plantings remain as urban development has encroached on this small appellation.

var:ratinho.name: Ratinho
var:ratinho.description: Ratinho is a rare white grape variety found in the Carcavelos region near Lisbon. It contributes to the traditional fortified wine blend of Carcavelos alongside Galego Dourado. Like Carcavelos itself, the grape is critically endangered.

var:tinta-miuda.name: Tinta Miúda
var:tinta-miuda.description: Tinta Miúda ('small dye' in Portuguese) is the Portuguese name for Graciano, a red grape variety shared with Spain. In Portugal, it produces concentrated, deeply colored wines with dark fruit and firm tannins. It is used as a blending component in several Portuguese DOCs, adding color and structure.

var:amaral.name: Amaral
var:amaral.description: Amaral is a red grape variety found in the Vinho Verde region of northern Portugal. It produces light-colored, fresh red wines — fitting the Vinho Verde style of lighter, refreshing wines. The grape is a minor variety in a region better known for its white wines.

var:cerceal.name: Cerceal
var:cerceal.description: Cerceal (or Cercial) is a white grape variety found in several Portuguese wine regions, including Dão and Bairrada. It produces crisp, mineral-driven white wines with good acidity. Not to be confused with Sercial of Madeira (a different grape), Cerceal is valued for freshness in Portuguese white blends.
var:cerceal.style: Crisp Portuguese white, valued for acidity

var:crato-branco.name: Crato Branco
var:crato-branco.description: Crato Branco is a white grape variety grown in the Algarve wine region of southern Portugal. It produces neutral, light-bodied white wines. The grape is adapted to the hot, dry conditions of the Algarve and is used in local DOC wines.

var:zierfandler.name: Zierfandler
var:zierfandler.description: Zierfandler (also known as Spätrot) is a white grape variety grown almost exclusively in the Thermenregion south of Vienna. Together with Rotgipfler, it produces the region's signature blends. Zierfandler contributes aromatic intensity, with spice and stone fruit notes. The name 'Spätrot' refers to the late-ripening berries turning reddish-pink. It is one of Austria's rarest and most distinctive indigenous varieties.
var:zierfandler.style: Rich, aromatic Thermenregion white, Austria's rarest specialty

var:rotgipfler.name: Rotgipfler
var:rotgipfler.description: Rotgipfler is a white grape variety found almost exclusively in the Thermenregion of Austria, south of Vienna. It produces full-bodied, aromatic white wines with notes of exotic fruit and spice. Rotgipfler is traditionally blended with Zierfandler to create the Thermenregion's most distinctive wines, though varietal bottlings exist. The name refers to the reddish tips of the vine shoots.
var:rotgipfler.style: Full-bodied Thermenregion white, Zierfandler's traditional partner

var:roter-veltliner.name: Roter Veltliner
var:roter-veltliner.description: Roter Veltliner is a white grape variety (despite 'Roter'/red in its name, referring to the berry color) grown in Austria's Wagram, Kamptal, and Weinviertel regions. Genetically unrelated to Grüner Veltliner, it produces rich, spicy white wines with good body and complexity. It is a niche variety appreciated for its distinctiveness.
var:roter-veltliner.style: Rich, spicy Austrian white — unrelated to Grüner Veltliner

geo:it:montepulciano-dabruzzo-colline-te.name: Montepulciano d'Abruzzo Colline Teramane
geo:it:montepulciano-dabruzzo-colline-te.description: Montepulciano d'Abruzzo Colline Teramane is a DOCG appellation in the Teramo province of Abruzzo, central Italy, elevated to DOCG status in 2010. It is a sub-zone of the broader Montepulciano d'Abruzzo DOC, restricted to hillside vineyards (colline means hills) in the Teramo hills. The appellation requires a minimum 90% Montepulciano grape and imposes stricter production rules than the parent DOC, including lower yields and mandatory aging. Wines tend to show greater structure, concentration, and aging potential compared to the broader DOC.
geo:it:montepulciano-dabruzzo-colline-te.style: Full-bodied red wine with deep color, firm tannins, and dark fruit; structured for aging

geo:fr:bourgogne-cote-d-or.name: Bourgogne Côte d'Or
geo:fr:bourgogne-cote-d-or.description: Bourgogne Côte d'Or is an AOC located within Burgundy's Côte d'Or escarpment, created by INAO in 2017. It encompasses wines from both the Côte de Nuits and Côte de Beaune, produced from Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. The designation replaces the generic Bourgogne label for wines from the Côte d'Or département, offering a clearer geographic signal than regional Bourgogne while sitting below village-level appellations.
geo:fr:bourgogne-cote-d-or.style: Structured red and white Burgundy from the Côte d'Or escarpment

geo:us:sierra-foothills.name: Sierra Foothills
geo:us:sierra-foothills.description: Sierra Foothills is a California AVA established in 1987, spanning the western slopes of the Sierra Nevada across Amador, El Dorado, Calaveras, Tuolumne, and Mariposa counties. Elevations from 1,200 to 3,500 feet above sea level create wide diurnal temperature swings that preserve acidity. The region's Gold Rush history left a legacy of old-vine Zinfandel, and the granitic and volcanic soils also support Syrah, Barbera, and Grenache.
geo:us:sierra-foothills.style: Bold, high-elevation California reds led by old-vine Zinfandel

geo:us:el-dorado.name: El Dorado
geo:us:el-dorado.description: El Dorado is a California AVA established in 1983 within the Sierra Foothills, covering El Dorado County at elevations of 1,200 to 3,500 feet. Named for the Gold Rush era, it is among California's highest-altitude wine regions. Granitic soils and wide day-night temperature variation produce reds with natural structure. Zinfandel dominates historically, with Syrah and Cabernet Sauvignon showing strong results at altitude.
geo:us:el-dorado.style: High-altitude Sierra Foothills reds with natural structure and cool-acidity

geo:cl:aconcagua-costa.name: Aconcagua Costa
geo:cl:aconcagua-costa.description: Aconcagua Costa is a sub-region of Chile's Aconcagua DO, occupying the coastal zone where the Pacific Ocean exerts a dominant influence. Persistent morning fog and cool ocean winds hold temperatures low relative to the inland Aconcagua Valley. The result is a cool-climate profile within a traditionally warm DO, favouring aromatic whites and delicate Pinot Noir rather than the heavier reds of the valley floor.
geo:cl:aconcagua-costa.style: Cool-climate coastal whites and Pinot Noir under Pacific influence

geo:ca:nova-scotia.name: Nova Scotia
geo:ca:nova-scotia.description: Nova Scotia is Canada's Atlantic wine region, influenced by the Bay of Fundy and the cold Labrador Current. The cool maritime climate and a long, slow growing season suit hybrid and cold-hardy vinifera varieties. L'Acadie Blanc is the flagship white, often appearing in the Tidal Bay VQA designation — an aromatic, fresh style unique to the province. The region also produces Riesling, Chardonnay, and small amounts of Pinot Noir.
geo:ca:nova-scotia.style: Cool maritime whites led by L'Acadie Blanc and Tidal Bay

geo:ca:beamsville-bench.name: Beamsville Bench
geo:ca:beamsville-bench.description: Beamsville Bench is a VQA sub-appellation within Ontario's Niagara Peninsula, situated on the elevated limestone Niagara Escarpment above Lake Ontario. The bench position provides free-draining soils and reliable sun exposure, while lake moderation extends the ripening window. Riesling and Chardonnay are the benchmark whites; Cabernet Franc and Pinot Noir succeed in warmer years. Several of Niagara's most acclaimed estate wineries are based here.
geo:ca:beamsville-bench.style: Structured Riesling and Chardonnay from the Niagara Escarpment limestone bench

geo:ca:lincoln-lakeshore.name: Lincoln Lakeshore
geo:ca:lincoln-lakeshore.description: Lincoln Lakeshore is a VQA sub-appellation along the Lake Ontario shoreline within the Niagara Peninsula. Lower elevation than Beamsville Bench brings more direct lake influence, moderating both summer heat and winter cold. The relatively warmer lower terraces favour red-grape ripening; Cabernet Franc and Merlot are common alongside Riesling in a softer, earlier-drinking style than the escarpment sub-appellations.
geo:ca:lincoln-lakeshore.style: Lake-moderated Niagara reds and whites in a rounder, approachable style

geo:it:chiaretto-di-bardolino.name: Chiaretto di Bardolino
geo:it:chiaretto-di-bardolino.description: Chiaretto di Bardolino is a DOC appellation on the eastern shore of Lake Garda in the Veneto, producing pale rosé from the same Corvina-led blend used for Bardolino DOC red. The two designations share geography but are legally distinct: Chiaretto must be vinified as rosé, producing a lighter extract and a fresh, delicate style. Lake Garda's moderating presence and the well-drained moraine soils of the shoreline contribute to the wine's characteristic pale colour and floral character.
geo:it:chiaretto-di-bardolino.style: Pale, delicate Lake Garda rosé from Corvina-led blend

var:charbono.name: Charbono
var:charbono.description: Charbono is a red grape variety. Alpine/Italian origin (synonym of Douce Noire/Bonarda); in California, old-vine survivor from Napa's earliest Italian plantings Medium to full-bodied, rustic yet approachable tannins; dark cherry and plum fruit; earthy, spicy character distinct from Argentine Bonarda Not to be confused with Argentine Bonarda, although botanically related. In California context, Charbono is used exclusively for this variety.

var:krakhuna.name: Krakhuna
var:krakhuna.description: Krakhuna is a white grape variety. Indigenous Georgian white grape from Imereti region; used in both qvevri skin-contact amber wines and conventional white production Full-bodied, broad; skin-contact versions show amber colour, dried apricot, walnut, and phenolic texture; conventional versions lighter with stone fruit Core Imeretian variety alongside Tsolikouri; often blended or used in traditional 3-6 day skin maceration in qvevri clay vessels

var:oseleta.name: Oseleta
var:oseleta.description: Oseleta is a red grape variety. Ancient Veronese variety from the Veneto; nearly extinct before recovery in the 1980s by Masi Agricola Intensely dark, highly tannic, rich in polyphenols; used as a powerful blending component in Amarone and Valpolicella; monovarietally produces dense, long-lived wines

var:otskhanuri-sapere.name: Otskhanuri Sapere
var:otskhanuri-sapere.description: Otskhanuri Sapere is a red grape variety. Indigenous Georgian red grape from Imereti and Racha-Lechkhumi; name translates roughly as 'fragrant from Otskhana' Medium-bodied structured reds with dark fruit and herbal notes; earthy character; both modern and traditional qvevri vinification used One of Georgia's lesser-known indigenous reds gaining international attention alongside Saperavi; demonstrates depth of Georgia's ancient grape heritage

var:tsitska.name: Tsitska
var:tsitska.description: Tsitska is a white grape variety. Indigenous Georgian white from Imereti; often blended with Tsolikouri in Imeretian whites Light-bodied, high acid, delicate citrus and floral notes; in Imeretian skin-contact wines adds freshness and aromatic lift Widely used in traditional Imeretian skin-contact wines (3-6 day maceration); the acidity makes it a natural blending partner for the fuller Tsolikouri

var:schiava.name: Schiava
var:schiava.description: Schiava is a red grape variety. Alto Adige (South Tyrol), Italy; also known as Vernatsch in German-speaking South Tyrol and as Trollinger in Württemberg, Germany Pale, light-bodied, low-tannin red with bright cherry and almond character; best served cool and young; approachable, food-friendly Schiava Gentile, Schiava Grossa, and Schiava Grigia are the three sub-varieties; Vernatsch is the traditional German name; Trollinger is the Württemberg synonym

ctr:tn.name: Tunisia
ctr:tn.description: Tunisia produces wine from ancient Phoenician-era traditions, with modern appellations concentrated around Mornag, Cap Bon, and the northern coastal belt. Mediterranean climate and limestone soils support Carignan, Cinsault, and Grenache for reds; Muscat de Kelibia is the flagship white, a naturally aromatic dessert and dry style from Cap Bon.
ctr:tn.style: Mediterranean reds and aromatic Muscat whites

geo:tn:mornag.name: Mornag
geo:tn:mornag.description: Mornag is the best-known Tunisian AOC, situated on a limestone plateau northeast of Tunis. Dominated by Carignan, Cinsault, and Grenache, the appellation produces structured red wines under a warm Mediterranean climate. The Société Vinicole de Mornag is the principal producer, with the Magon brand among Tunisia's most exported labels.
geo:tn:mornag.style: Warm-climate Mediterranean reds, structured and food-friendly

geo:tn:muscat-de-kelibia.name: Muscat de Kelibia
geo:tn:muscat-de-kelibia.description: Muscat de Kelibia occupies the Cap Bon peninsula on Tunisia's northeast coast, where the Muscat Sec de Kelibia grape — an ancient local clone — produces both sweet and dry whites of floral intensity. The cool sea breezes moderate summer heat, preserving aromatic freshness. Tunisia's only internationally recognised white AOC.
geo:tn:muscat-de-kelibia.style: Fragrant sweet and dry whites; floral, fresh, aromatic

geo:ca:quebec.name: Quebec
geo:ca:quebec.description: Quebec's wine industry is built around cold-hardy hybrid varieties — Vidal, Frontenac, Marquette — bred to survive harsh winters. Wineries cluster along the St. Lawrence south shore and Eastern Townships. The short growing season rewards aromatic whites and ice wine styles, while red grapes like Frontenac push into structured territory.
geo:ca:quebec.style: Cold-climate hybrids; aromatic whites, ice wine, light reds

geo:ca:british-columbia.name: British Columbia
geo:ca:british-columbia.description: British Columbia's wine regions span the Okanagan Valley desert, the cooler Similkameen, and coastal Gulf Islands. The Okanagan's long summer days and cool nights suit Merlot, Cabernet Franc, and Syrah in the south, with Riesling and Pinot Gris thriving further north. BC holds Canada's largest fine-wine concentration outside Niagara.
geo:ca:british-columbia.style: Variable; desert Merlot in the south, aromatic whites in the north

geo:ca:ontario.name: Ontario
geo:ca:ontario.description: Ontario is Canada's largest wine province, with the Niagara Peninsula at its core moderated by Lake Ontario and the Niagara Escarpment. The lake effect prevents extreme cold, enabling Riesling, Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Cabernet Franc to ripen in the Tender Fruit Belt. Prince Edward County offers a cooler Burgundian-style terroir. Ontario's VQA system governs the appellation structure.
geo:ca:ontario.style: Finesse-driven whites and Pinot Noir; notable ice wine tradition

geo:ca:vqa-niagara-peninsula.name: VQA Niagara Peninsula
geo:ca:vqa-niagara-peninsula.description: The VQA Niagara Peninsula designation anchors Ontario fine-wine production between Lake Ontario and Lake Erie. The Niagara Escarpment acts as a thermal shield, trapping cool lake air and enabling Riesling, Chardonnay, and Pinot Noir to develop complexity. Benchmarks include Riesling, Cabernet Franc, and the world's finest Vidal Icewine.
geo:ca:vqa-niagara-peninsula.style: Elegant Riesling and Chardonnay; Vidal Icewine benchmark

geo:gr:aegean-islands.name: Aegean Islands
geo:gr:aegean-islands.description: The Aegean Islands scatter wine production across Santorini, Lesbos, Lemnos, Samos, and Chios. Volcanic Santorini, with its basket-trained Assyrtiko vines and pumice soils, is the signature. Muscat-dominant Samos and Lemnos produce sweet and dry whites. The island terroirs are characterised by intense sun, marine winds, and indigenous varieties.
geo:gr:aegean-islands.style: Mineral volcanic whites (Santorini), aromatic Muscat; island-character driven

geo:gr:attica.name: Attica
geo:gr:attica.description: Attica surrounds Athens and has been wine country since antiquity. The Mesogeia plain east of the capital is Savvatiano's heartland — the grape that made Retsina. Modern producers have shifted toward clean varietal Savvatiano and international blends, but Retsina from the Mesogeia remains a living link to the ancient practice of adding Aleppo pine resin to stabilise wine.
geo:gr:attica.style: Savvatiano whites, including traditional pine-resin Retsina

geo:gr:retsina-of-mesogia.name: Retsina of Mesogia
geo:gr:retsina-of-mesogia.description: Retsina of Mesogia is the PGI designation for the Attica Mesogeia plain, where Savvatiano grapes are fermented with a controlled addition of Aleppo pine resin. The result is a dry white with a distinctive resinous, herbal note that softens with the dish it was historically paired with — seafood and meze. PGI status codifies resin sourcing and dosage.
geo:gr:retsina-of-mesogia.style: Dry resinous white; herbal, saline, food-specific tradition

geo:it:colli-toscana-centrale.name: Colli Toscana Centrale
geo:it:colli-toscana-centrale.description: Colli Toscana Centrale is a broad Tuscan IGT covering the central Apennine foothills between Florence and Siena. Established to accommodate the Super-Tuscan movement, it permits Sangiovese blended with Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and other international varieties outside the Chianti classification. Estates such as Ornellaia and Sassicaia use neighbouring IGTs, but this designation spans a wide productive zone.
geo:it:colli-toscana-centrale.style: Super-Tuscan blends; structured, internationally styled reds

geo:it:rosso-verona.name: Rosso Verona
geo:it:rosso-verona.description: Rosso Verona is an IGT designation for red wines produced in the Verona province outside the stricter Valpolicella, Bardolino, or Soave DOC/DOCG frameworks. Corvina, Rondinella, and Molinara — the classic Veronese trio — dominate, often with permitted international additions. It provides flexibility for producers seeking to declassify or blend more freely within the regional identity.
geo:it:rosso-verona.style: Veronese reds; approachable, fruit-forward, medium weight

geo:it:st-magdalener-classico.name: St. Magdalener Classico
geo:it:st-magdalener-classico.description: St. Magdalener Classico (Santa Maddalena Classico) is an Alto Adige DOC on sun-drenched hillsides above Bolzano. Schiava Gentile dominates, producing light-bodied reds with distinctive almond, cherry, and violet aromatics. The classico subzone represents the historic core, farmed on steep volcanic porphyry terraces at 300–600m. Once among Italy's most celebrated reds.
geo:it:st-magdalener-classico.style: Light, perfumed red; almond, cherry, violet; Alpine freshness

geo:cl:coquimbo.name: Coquimbo
geo:cl:coquimbo.description: Coquimbo is Chile's northernmost wine region, encompassing the Elqui and Limarí valleys on the edge of the Atacama. The extreme aridity, altitude, and Pacific fog combine to produce strikingly mineral Syrah and Chardonnay. Limarí's calcium carbonate soils give its whites particular chalky precision. A frontier zone for climate-adapted viticulture.
geo:cl:coquimbo.style: Mineral whites and high-altitude Syrah; cool despite latitude

geo:cl:san-antonio-casablanca.name: San Antonio - Casablanca
geo:cl:san-antonio-casablanca.description: San Antonio - Casablanca is an inter-regional Chilean designation linking two adjacent cool-climate coastal valleys. Cold Humboldt Current upwelling generates morning fog and afternoon breezes that keep growing temperatures low. Sauvignon Blanc achieves grassy, citrus precision; Pinot Noir develops elegance at a latitude that surprises for its freshness.
geo:cl:san-antonio-casablanca.style: Cool-climate coastal whites; crisp Sauvignon Blanc, elegant Pinot

geo:au:limestone-coast.name: Limestone Coast
geo:au:limestone-coast.description: Limestone Coast is a South Australian GI zone spanning Coonawarra, Padthaway, Wrattonbully, and several other districts atop a calcium-carbonate plateau south-east of Adelaide. Terra rossa over limestone is the defining soil, amplifying Cabernet Sauvignon's regional signature in Coonawarra — often described as Australia's most distinctive red-wine soil. Shiraz and Chardonnay also excel across the zone.
geo:au:limestone-coast.style: Terra rossa reds; structured Cabernet, vibrant Shiraz, cool-inflected whites

geo:au:north-east-victoria.name: North East Victoria
geo:au:north-east-victoria.description: North East Victoria encompasses the King Valley, Milawa, and Rutherglen districts along the Alpine foothills. Rutherglen is synonymous with fortified Muscat and Topaque of extraordinary richness. Milawa and King Valley produce aromatic whites — Riesling, Prosecco, and Gewurztraminer — at altitude. The region maintains one of Australia's most diverse stylistic ranges.
geo:au:north-east-victoria.style: Rich fortified Muscat (Rutherglen), aromatic Alpine whites, Prosecco

geo:au:milawa.name: Milawa
geo:au:milawa.description: Milawa is a GI subregion in North East Victoria, home to Brown Brothers — a family winery with over 130 years of history founded in 1889. The cool high-altitude sites in nearby Whitlands grow Riesling and Chardonnay of precision; the Milawa floor suits warmer varieties. Milawa's experimental Kindergarten Vineyard launched many Australian plantings of southern Italian and Portuguese varieties.
geo:au:milawa.style: Aromatic whites, experimental variety range; founded 1889 heritage

geo:fr:macon-la-roche-vineuse.name: Mâcon La Roche Vineuse
geo:fr:macon-la-roche-vineuse.description: Mâcon La Roche Vineuse is a village-level Mâcon AOC whose name translates as 'the stony vineyard,' reflecting its characteristic limestone and clay soil profile. Chardonnay grown here acquires a taut mineral structure alongside ripe orchard fruit, offering village-level precision at Mâcon pricing. Olivier Merlin and Domaine de la Bongran are key estates.
geo:fr:macon-la-roche-vineuse.style: Mineral Chardonnay; structured, fresh, village-level Mâconnais

geo:fr:macon-chardonnay.name: Mâcon-Chardonnay
geo:fr:macon-chardonnay.description: Mâcon-Chardonnay is the village appellation of Chardonnay — the commune in the Mâconnais that gave the world its most planted white grape variety. Local legend holds that the grape was identified and named here in the Middle Ages. Wines are Chardonnay-only, typically expressing a crisp, fresh style with limestone minerality and light stone-fruit character.
geo:fr:macon-chardonnay.style: Crisp, mineral Chardonnay; historically the namesake village of the variety

geo:ge:manavi.name: Manavi
geo:ge:manavi.description: Manavi is a Georgian PDO in the Kakheti region, devoted to Mtsvane Kakhuri — a white grape producing wines of distinctive floral intensity, citrus freshness, and light body. The PDO sits on the alluvial fan of the Alazani river at the foot of the Caucasus, where warm days and cool nights preserve aromatic precision. Manavi white was historically served at Georgian royal banquets.
geo:ge:manavi.style: Aromatic dry white; floral, citrus, light-bodied Kakheti character

geo:uy:progreso.name: Progreso
geo:uy:progreso.description: Progreso is a Uruguayan wine-producing department west of Montevideo, historically one of the country's earliest viticultural settlements. Tannat, transplanted by Basque immigrants in the late 19th century, dominates alongside Merlot. The clay-loam soils and Atlantic-influenced climate produce structured, earthy reds with characteristic tannic grip — a more rustic counterpart to the coastal Maldonado style.

geo:za:coastal-region.name: Coastal Region
geo:za:coastal-region.description: The Coastal Region is a broad South African WO designation covering the Atlantic and Indian Ocean-facing districts from Stellenbosch and Swartland to Paarl and Franschhoek. Maritime winds, diverse soils, and steep mountain slopes create micro-climatic complexity. It functions as both a qualitative anchor for individual WO district wines and an accessible umbrella for blended regional production.
geo:za:coastal-region.style: Varied; world-class Cabernet and Syrah from cool mountain sites

geo:za:mcgregor.name: McGregor
geo:za:mcgregor.description: McGregor is a WO ward in the Robertson valley, sheltered by the Riviersonderend Mountains. Co-operative production dominates in this hot inland zone, with Muscadel (Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains) its most distinctive product — fortified, golden, and intensely sweet. Colombard and Chardonnay also feature in the unfortified white production.
geo:za:mcgregor.style: Fortified Muscadel speciality; hot-valley whites, co-operative driven

var:temjanika.name: Temjanika
var:temjanika.description: Temjanika (Serbian: Tamjanika) is an aromatic white grape of the Muscat family, closely related to Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains and to Romania's Tămâioasă Românească. Its name evokes incense — a nod to its perfumed character. In North Macedonia it is a signature white of the Tikveš region, giving light-bodied, crisp wines with intense Muscat aromatics of white flowers, citrus, and stone fruit. Best enjoyed young and well chilled.
var:temjanika.style: Light-bodied, intensely aromatic white with Muscat floral character

geo:it:barolo-cannubi.name: Cannubi
geo:it:barolo-cannubi.description: Cannubi is the most celebrated named vineyard (MGA) in the Barolo DOCG, codified in 2007 on the gently south-southeast facing central slope of the Barolo commune at 230–280 m altitude. Soils are a distinctive Tortonian–Helvetian mix of calcareous marl (Saint Agatha Fossil Marl) that produces Nebbiolo of aromatic finesse and early elegance rather than the raw tannic power of Serralunga. The 2007 codification resolved the Cannubi hill into distinct sub-MGAs — Cannubi, Cannubi Boschis, Cannubi Muscatel, Cannubi San Lorenzo, and Cannubi Valletta — while retaining the umbrella 'Cannubi' labelling right for the full hill. Luciano Sandrone's Cannubi Boschis bottling (now labelled Aleste) and bottlings from Francesco Rinaldi and Brovia define the benchmark for this cru.
geo:it:barolo-cannubi.style: Elegant, perfumed Barolo; aromatic finesse over brute power

geo:it:barolo-cannubi-boschis.name: Cannubi Boschis
geo:it:barolo-cannubi-boschis.description: Cannubi Boschis is an officially codified sub-MGA within the Cannubi hill in the Barolo DOCG, sitting on the northern-facing section above the core Cannubi slope in the commune of Barolo. The 2007 MGA codification distinguished it from the broader Cannubi with its own designated name, reflecting a slightly cooler, more shaded aspect that adds freshness and floral lift to the Nebbiolo. Luciano Sandrone's estate bottling — formerly labelled Cannubi Boschis, since renamed Aleste — made this sub-MGA internationally famous and remains its defining reference.
geo:it:barolo-cannubi-boschis.style: Floral, fresh Barolo with Cannubi-family elegance

geo:it:barolo-brunate.name: Brunate
geo:it:barolo-brunate.description: Brunate straddles the communes of La Morra and Barolo on a prominent south-facing hillcrest reaching 350–400 m, making it one of the highest-altitude major MGAs in the denomination. The Tortonian Helvetian calcareous marl soils of its La Morra portion yield Nebbiolo of perfumed, violet-inflected character and relatively open structure; the Barolo-commune parcel adds more grip. Roberto Voerzio, Vietti, G.B. Burlotto, and Oddero are the benchmark producers, each capturing a slightly different facet of Brunate's renowned aromatic expressiveness.
geo:it:barolo-brunate.style: Aromatic, medium-to-full bodied Barolo; perfumed and age-worthy

geo:it:barolo-cerequio.name: Cerequio
geo:it:barolo-cerequio.description: Cerequio occupies a broad amphitheatre-shaped south-southwest slope straddling La Morra and Barolo communes at 280–350 m, with excellent sun exposure throughout the growing season. Tortonian calcareous marl soils produce Barolo of generous fruit, perfume, and relatively supple tannin — the La Morra hallmark of earlier accessibility within the DOCG. Roberto Voerzio's Cerequio is the defining reference bottling; Michele Chiarlo is another longstanding Cerequio house.
geo:it:barolo-cerequio.style: Broad, perfumed Barolo; generosity and approachability

geo:it:barolo-rocche-dell-annunziata.name: Rocche dell'Annunziata
geo:it:barolo-rocche-dell-annunziata.description: Rocche dell'Annunziata is one of La Morra's most prized MGAs, an east-to-south-facing slope at 280–330 m distinguished by the rocky outcroppings ('rocche') that mark its upper ridge. Tortonian Helvetian soils grant the Nebbiolo here classic La Morra perfume — violet, tar, and red fruit — with a silky texture that repays medium-term ageing. Roberto Voerzio (Torriglione) and Paolo Scavino (Riserva) are the flagship producers, with Trediberri and Aurelio Settimo also bottling this cru.
geo:it:barolo-rocche-dell-annunziata.style: Silky, perfumed La Morra Barolo; violet and red fruit

geo:it:barolo-la-serra.name: La Serra
geo:it:barolo-la-serra.description: La Serra is a south-facing ridge MGA in La Morra commune at 300–370 m, positioned on the backbone of the hill directly above Brunate. Tortonian soils here are lighter and more well-drained than lower La Morra MGAs, yielding Nebbiolo with pronounced aromatic lift, firm but refined tannin, and good ageing capacity. Roberto Voerzio has bottled La Serra for decades and defines its benchmark; Marcarini is the other historic estate associated with this cru.
geo:it:barolo-la-serra.style: Aromatic, structured La Morra Barolo; lifted and age-worthy

geo:it:barolo-monprivato.name: Monprivato
geo:it:barolo-monprivato.description: Monprivato is an east-to-southeast facing slope MGA in Castiglione Falletto at 280–310 m, effectively a monopole of Giuseppe Mascarello e Figlio, who has farmed and bottled it for generations. The Helvetian soils — compact Serravallian marl with higher clay content — produce Nebbiolo of considerable structure, dark fruit, iron minerality, and outstanding longevity; these are among the most cellar-worthy Barolos made. Mascarello's Barolo Monprivato and the Riserva Cà d'Morissio (from the oldest vines) set the global reference for this cru.
geo:it:barolo-monprivato.style: Structured, cellar-worthy Barolo; dark fruit, iron, exceptional longevity

geo:it:barolo-villero.name: Villero
geo:it:barolo-villero.description: Villero is a south-southwest facing MGA in Castiglione Falletto at 270–320 m, with Helvetian calcareous marl soils that produce some of the commune's most structured Nebbiolo. The wines combine the aromatic finesse associated with Castiglione's central position with the tannic backbone of a Serravallian-influenced subsoil, yielding Barolo that is powerful yet graceful. Giuseppe Mascarello, Brovia, and Vietti (Villero Riserva) are the benchmark producers and between them demonstrate the traditional and modern range of this cru.
geo:it:barolo-villero.style: Powerful, graceful Barolo; structured with aromatic finesse

geo:it:barolo-rocche-di-castiglione.name: Rocche di Castiglione
geo:it:barolo-rocche-di-castiglione.description: Rocche di Castiglione is a southeast-facing MGA on the terraced escarpment above Castiglione Falletto village at 300–360 m, receiving some of the best morning sun in the commune. Helvetian soils with compact marl and good drainage produce Nebbiolo of great concentration, prominent tannin, and a distinctive iron-mineral quality that distinguishes Castiglione Falletto from La Morra. Vietti's Rocche di Castiglione is the single-cru flagship; Brovia and Oddero add further benchmark bottlings. The name was formally confirmed as 'Rocche di Castiglione' in the 2007 MGA codification.
geo:it:barolo-rocche-di-castiglione.style: Concentrated, tannic Barolo; iron minerality and depth

geo:it:barolo-bricco-boschis.name: Bricco Boschis
geo:it:barolo-bricco-boschis.description: Bricco Boschis ('wooded hilltop') is a high-altitude south-to-southwest facing MGA in Castiglione Falletto at 310–350 m, dominated by the Cavallotto estate, which holds the majority of the vineyard. The higher elevation and Helvetian Serravallian soils extend the ripening season, producing Nebbiolo of refined, complex aromatics and firm tannic structure with excellent longevity. Cavallotto's Bricco Boschis bottlings — including a celebrated Vigna San Giuseppe Riserva — are the de facto reference for this near-monopole cru.

geo:it:barolo-vignane.name: Vignane
geo:it:barolo-vignane.description: Vignane is a south-facing MGA in the Barolo commune at 250–300 m, producing Nebbiolo of characteristic Barolo-commune elegance and relatively lighter body compared to the Serralunga style. Tortonian calcareous marl soils and a sheltered position contribute to wines that are perfumed and approachable. Brovia is the best-known producer bottling from Vignane, one of the lower-profile but legitimate MGAs within the Barolo-commune tier.

geo:it:barolo-sarmassa.name: Sarmassa
geo:it:barolo-sarmassa.description: Sarmassa is a south-facing MGA in the Barolo commune at 260–320 m, known for its full-bodied, perfumed Nebbiolo that balances Barolo-commune elegance with genuine structural depth. The vineyard sits on Tortonian calcareous marl, producing wines with generous aromatics — violet, rose, red cherry — and a firm but polished tannic framework. Roberto Voerzio and Marchesi di Barolo are the two principal producers defining the benchmark for this cru.
geo:it:barolo-sarmassa.style: Full, perfumed Barolo; aromatic generosity with structural depth

geo:it:barolo-bussia.name: Bussia
geo:it:barolo-bussia.description: Bussia is one of Monforte d'Alba's largest and most important MGAs, a broad southeast-to-southwest facing amphitheatre at 280–400 m that encompasses several famous sub-vineyards including Romirasco, Cicala, and Colonnello. Helvetian Serravallian marl soils — compact, rich in minerals, and well-drained — give the Nebbiolo of Bussia its hallmark power, depth, and longevity: the Monforte signature of concentration over early charm. Poderi Aldo Conterno is the defining estate (Granbussia Riserva is a Barolo icon); Prunotto and Oddero (Vigna Mondoca) add further benchmark single-cru bottlings.
geo:it:barolo-bussia.style: Powerful, deep Barolo; Monforte concentration and longevity

geo:it:barolo-ginestra.name: Ginestra
geo:it:barolo-ginestra.description: Ginestra is a south-southeast facing MGA in Monforte d'Alba at 270–380 m, one of the commune's most expressive terroirs for powerful, aromatic Barolo. Serravallian marl soils with good mineral content produce Nebbiolo of deep colour, persistent tar-and-roses complexity, and the long tannic structure that demands a decade or more of cellaring. Elio Grasso (Casa Maté), Domenico Clerico (Ciabot Mentin), Paolo Scavino, and Giacomo Fenocchio each bottle a distinct facet of Ginestra's concentrated, age-worthy character.
geo:it:barolo-ginestra.style: Powerful, tannic Barolo; tar, roses, deep Monforte concentration

geo:it:barolo-mosconi.name: Mosconi
geo:it:barolo-mosconi.description: Mosconi is a south-facing MGA adjacent to Ginestra in Monforte d'Alba at 280–360 m, sharing the commune's Serravallian marl soils and tendency toward concentrated, structured Barolo. The wines exhibit dark fruit, earthy minerality, and the firm tannin typical of Monforte, requiring patient cellaring to show their best. Giacomo Fenocchio's Barolo Mosconi (including the 90 Dì Riserva), Pio Cesare, and Conterno-Fantino are the principal estates working this cru.
geo:it:barolo-mosconi.style: Concentrated, earthy Barolo; Monforte structure and patience required

geo:it:barolo-mariondino.name: Mariondino
geo:it:barolo-mariondino.description: Mariondino is a south-facing MGA in Castiglione Falletto at 260–300 m, situated near the Monprivato boundary and sharing broadly similar Helvetian marl soils with good drainage. The wines tend toward the firm, mineral Castiglione Falletto style — tannic framework with good aromatic presence — though the MGA has lower visibility than its famous neighbours. Giacomo Fenocchio is among the producers with holdings in the Mariondino area.
geo:it:barolo-mariondino.style: Firm, mineral Barolo; Castiglione Falletto structure

geo:it:barolo-vigna-rionda.name: Vigna Rionda
geo:it:barolo-vigna-rionda.description: Vigna Rionda (officially codified as 'Vignarionda' in 2007) is Serralunga d'Alba's most prestigious MGA, a steep south-to-southwest facing site at 300–380 m with pure Helvetian Serravallian marl soils. The combination of altitude, aspect, and compact mineral-rich soil produces Nebbiolo of extraordinary concentration, firm tannic architecture, and exceptional longevity — the archetypal Serralunga Barolo. Massolino (Riserva), Oddero (Vignarionda 10 Anni), and Giovanni Rosso (Ester Canale Rosso) are the benchmarks; Bruno Giacosa's legendary Collina Rionda Riserva drew from the same hill.
geo:it:barolo-vigna-rionda.style: Austere, concentrated Barolo; Serralunga power and 20+ year potential

geo:it:barolo-falletto.name: Falletto
geo:it:barolo-falletto.description: Falletto is a south-to-southeast facing MGA in Serralunga d'Alba at 280–330 m, strongly identified with the Bruno Giacosa estate, whose Barolo Falletto and Barolo Le Rocche del Falletto Riserva have long been reference points for the appellation's full-throttle Serralunga style. Helvetian Serravallian marl soils produce Nebbiolo of intense concentration, dark fruit, tar, and ferrous minerality, requiring a decade of cellaring before approaching its peak. While not a full monopole, the MGA's identity is inseparable from Giacosa's decades of single-vineyard bottlings.
geo:it:barolo-falletto.style: Intense, concentrated Barolo; tar, dark fruit, long-lived Serralunga

geo:it:barolo-cerretta.name: Cerretta
geo:it:barolo-cerretta.description: Cerretta is a south-facing MGA in Serralunga d'Alba at 300–360 m, on the northern slope of the Serralunga ridge with Helvetian Serravallian soils delivering the commune's signature tannic power and mineral depth. The wine shows dark berry fruit, earthy notes, and firm tannin that softens with time into a complex, savoury expression — a quieter but quality-consistent Serralunga cru. Giovanni Rosso and Ceretto (Bricco Rocche estate) are the principal producers; note that the MGA 'Cerretta' (the place) is entirely distinct from the producer 'Ceretto' (the family name).
geo:it:barolo-cerretta.style: Dark-fruited, tannic Serralunga Barolo; earthy and mineral

geo:it:barolo-arione.name: Arione
geo:it:barolo-arione.description: Arione is a south-to-southwest facing MGA in Serralunga d'Alba at 270–330 m, acquired by Giacomo Conterno in 2015 to complement the estate's historic Francia holding on the same commune. Helvetian Serravallian marl soils give classic Serralunga power — dense, concentrated Nebbiolo with substantial tannic structure and the deep mineral character of this demanding terroir. Conterno's Barolo Arione is the sole current benchmark bottling and places this MGA within the upper tier of Serralunga production.
geo:it:barolo-arione.style: Dense, structured Serralunga Barolo; mineral depth and ageing potential

geo:it:barolo-lazzarito.name: Lazzarito
geo:it:barolo-lazzarito.description: Lazzarito is a south-to-southeast facing MGA in Serralunga d'Alba at 260–320 m, one of the commune's established single-vineyard names with Helvetian Serravallian soils that produce full-bodied, powerful Nebbiolo. The wines combine dark fruit, tobacco, and earthy minerality with the firm tannic structure intrinsic to Serralunga, requiring substantial cellaring to integrate. Fontanafredda (Vigna La Delizia) and Vietti are the benchmark producers, with Vietti's Barolo Lazzarito considered among the estate's finest Serralunga crus.
geo:it:barolo-lazzarito.style: Full-bodied, tobacco-edged Barolo; Serralunga power and structure

geo:it:barolo-francia.name: Francia
geo:it:barolo-francia.description: Francia is an official 2007 Barolo MGA in Serralunga d'Alba, broadly south-facing at 270–330 m, inseparably associated with Giacomo Conterno whose Cascina Francia farm has defined this terroir for generations. Helvetian Serravallian marl soils give the Nebbiolo of Francia its characteristic density, mineral backbone, and enormous longevity — Conterno's Barolo Francia and the Monfortino Riserva (a selection from the estate) are among Italy's most collectible wines. The MGA name 'Francia' is the official codified toponym; Cascina Francia is the Conterno estate name, and Monfortino is a riserva selection — neither is a separate MGA.
geo:it:barolo-francia.style: Dense, mineral Barolo; extraordinary longevity, Giacomo Conterno benchmark

geo:it:barolo-pira-serralunga.name: Pira
geo:it:barolo-pira-serralunga.description: Pira is an officially codified 2007 MGA in Serralunga d'Alba at 270–320 m, a south-facing site on the Serralunga ridge with compact Helvetian Serravallian marl soils. The terroir delivers the characteristic Serralunga profile of concentrated dark fruit, firm tannic structure, and mineral depth, with wines that require significant bottle age. The MGA is distinct from the Barolo-commune Pira holdings; several small producers work the site, with Luigi Pira the best-known estate historically associated with this cru.
geo:it:barolo-pira-serralunga.style: Structured, mineral Serralunga Barolo; concentrated and age-demanding

geo:it:barolo-monvigliero.name: Monvigliero
geo:it:barolo-monvigliero.description: Monvigliero is the flagship MGA of the Verduno commune, an east-to-south-facing hillside at 230–290 m that marks the northern extreme of the Barolo DOCG. The Tortonian Helvetian calcareous marl soils and the slightly cooler Verduno microclimate — the appellation's most northerly exposure — produce Nebbiolo of distinctive aromatic elegance, high-toned red fruit, and lifted floral character, typically lighter in body than Serralunga but intensely perfumed. Comm. G.B. Burlotto's whole-cluster foot-trodden Barolo Monvigliero is the defining reference; Castello di Verduno and Fratelli Alessandria are other benchmark Verduno estates.
geo:it:barolo-monvigliero.style: Aromatic, elegant Barolo; high-toned red fruit, Verduno floral lift

geo:it:barolo-ravera.name: Ravera
geo:it:barolo-ravera.description: Ravera is a south-to-southwest facing MGA largely within the Novello commune at 280–350 m, with a small portion extending into Barolo commune; one of the denomination's most highly regarded crus outside the Serralunga–La Morra axis. The mix of Tortonian and Helvetian marl — with clay-rich subsoil in the lower sections — produces Nebbiolo of considerable structure, dark berry aromatics, and a velvety texture that bridges La Morra's perfume with Monforte's power. G.D. Vajra and Elio Altare are the principal benchmarks; Vietti, Cogno, and Brovia also bottle this cru.
geo:it:barolo-ravera.style: Structured, velvety Barolo; dark berry, bridging perfume and power

geo:it:barolo-bricco-delle-viole.name: Bricco delle Viole
geo:it:barolo-bricco-delle-viole.description: Bricco delle Viole ('hilltop of violets') is the highest-altitude significant MGA in the Barolo commune, reaching 350–420 m on the steep south-to-southwest facing slopes above Barolo village. The extreme altitude delays ripening and intensifies aromatic complexity, with Tortonian Helvetian calcareous marl soils giving Nebbiolo of pronounced violet perfume (the vineyard's namesake), bright acidity, and refined tannic structure. G.D. Vajra's Barolo Bricco delle Viole is the defining bottling and gives the MGA its international reputation.
geo:it:barolo-bricco-delle-viole.style: Highly aromatic, refined Barolo; violet perfume, cool-climate precision

geo:it:barolo-pajana.name: Pajana
geo:it:barolo-pajana.description: Pajana is an officially codified 2007 MGA adjacent to Ginestra in Monforte d'Alba at 280–360 m, sharing the commune's Serravallian marl soils and tendency toward concentrated, structured Barolo. The terroir produces Nebbiolo of dark fruit, earthy depth, and the firm tannic backbone characteristic of the Monforte side of the appellation. Domenico Clerico's Barolo Pajana established the cru's reputation as a serious single-vineyard wine, though the estate is not yet in the evidence-grade producer corpus.
geo:it:barolo-pajana.style: Concentrated, earthy Barolo; Monforte power and tannic depth

geo:it:barolo-boscareto.name: Boscareto
geo:it:barolo-boscareto.description: Boscareto is a south-facing MGA in Serralunga d'Alba at 280–330 m, positioned on the western face of the Serralunga ridge with Helvetian Serravallian marl soils that produce the commune's characteristic powerful, austere Nebbiolo. The wines show dense dark fruit, firm tannin, and the mineral backbone of Serralunga, requiring patient ageing to open. Fontanafredda, with extensive Serralunga holdings, is the primary producer working this MGA.
geo:it:barolo-boscareto.style: Austere, structured Serralunga Barolo; dense and mineral

var:kraljevina.name: Kraljevina
var:kraljevina.description: An old indigenous white grape of the Croatian Uplands (Zagorje, Prigorje, Moslavina) that also crosses into Slovenian Posavje. It yields pale, low-alcohol, high-acid whites with gentle floral and citrus notes, and increasingly serves as a base for sparkling wine. In Slovenia it is a minor component of light red blends such as Cviček.
var:kraljevina.style: Crisp, low-alcohol, high-acid white; light and floral.

var:malvazija-istrska.name: Malvazija Istrska
var:malvazija-istrska.description: The signature white grape of the Istrian peninsula, shared across Slovenia, Croatia and Italian Friuli. Genetically distinct from the wider Malvasia family, it makes dry whites ranging from fresh, light and floral with a bitter-almond finish to structured, textural skin-contact (amber) styles. Marl and limestone soils lend a persistent minerality.
var:malvazija-istrska.style: Dry white, floral with an almond note; crisp to skin-contact.

var:plavec-zuti.name: Plavec Žuti
var:plavec-zuti.description: A high-acid, low-alcohol white of the Croatia–Slovenia border, known in Slovenia as Rumeni Plavec. Its tart, restrained fruit makes it a natural base for sparkling wine and a workhorse of the Bizeljsko-Brežice district. A 2020 study identifies it as a natural Gouais Blanc × Vulpea cross.
var:plavec-zuti.style: Tart, low-alcohol white; classic sparkling base.

var:zametovka.name: Žametovka
var:zametovka.description: An ancient red Slovenian variety (also called Modra Kavčina / Kavčina črna) and one of the country's oldest cultivars — the world's oldest fruiting vine, in Maribor, is Žametovka. It gives pale, light-bodied, high-acid reds and is the backbone of the traditional low-alcohol Cviček blend of Dolenjska.
var:zametovka.style: Pale, light, high-acid red; Cviček component.

var:pinela.name: Pinela
var:pinela.description: A rare indigenous white of Slovenia's Vipava Valley, documented since the 14th century and grown on only a few dozen hectares. It gives light, fresh, gently fruity whites (pear, subtle floral) with moderate acidity and a mineral edge from the valley's marl-limestone soils.
var:pinela.style: Light, mineral, gently fruity dry white.

var:refosk.name: Refošk
var:refosk.description: The dark, deeply pigmented red of Slovenia's Kras (Karst) and Istria, genetically distinct from Friuli's Refosco dal Peduncolo Rosso though part of the same Refosco family. Grown on iron-rich terra rossa, it makes high-acid, firmly tannic wines of dark berry and ferrous character — the grape behind the Kraški Teran PDO.

var:vitovska-grganja.name: Vitovska Grganja
var:vitovska-grganja.description: A native white of the Kras (Karst) plateau straddling Slovenia and Italian Carso. Well adapted to the wind-swept limestone, it makes dry whites with white-flower and pear aromatics, firm acidity and a saline mineral finish, and is a frequent choice for textural skin-contact (amber) wines.
var:vitovska-grganja.style: Mineral, floral, saline dry white; often skin-contact.

var:zelen.name: Zelen
var:zelen.description: A semi-aromatic indigenous white found almost exclusively in Slovenia's Vipava Valley, named for the emerald tint of its must. It gives straw-green wines with pear and apple fruit, herbal lift and medium acidity. A rarity — under 80 hectares — pulled back from near-extinction in the early 2000s.
